###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Military Ranks/Computers : WAS Re: newbie question on cobol syntax

_(thread depth: 32)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2007-05-09T19:03:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f1t5tu$8sm$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<bklp235d03auknn51jp1rt477h2fhmk6dc@4ax.com> <1178651948.257484.39320@y5g2000hsa.googlegroups.com> <f1s3g7$s30$1@reader2.panix.com> <1178736612.417596.27430@y80g2000hsf.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <1178736612.417596.27430@y80g2000hsf.googlegroups.com>,
Alistair  <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>On 9 May, 10:15, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:
>> In article <1178651948.257484.39...@y5g2000hsa.googlegroups.com>,
…[47 more quoted lines elided]…
>[53:21]   Do you have sons, while He has these as daughters?

Mr Maclean, I do not know what to make of the scholarship of the sources 
posting at vexen.co.uk ... but a stroll over to 
http://www.submission.org/suras/sura53.html places these verses in what 
seems to be an entirely different context:

--begin quoted text:

[the Prophet speaks of a Divine Revelation of Paradise]

[53:13] He saw him in another descent. 

[53:14] At the ultimate point. 

[53:15] Where the eternal Paradise is located. 

[53:16] The whole place was overwhelmed. 

[53:17] The eyes did not waver, nor go blind. 

[53:18] He saw great signs of his Lord. 

The Flimsy Idols 

[53:19] Compare this with the female idols Allaat and Al-`Uzzah. 

[53:20] And Manaat, the third one. 

[53:21] Do you have sons, while He has these as daughters? 

[53:22] What a disgraceful distribution! 

[53:23] These are but names that you made up, you and your forefathers. 
GOD never authorized such a blasphemy. They follow conjecture, and 
personal desire, when the true guidance has come to them herein from their 
Lord.

[53:24] What is it that the human being desires? 

[53:25] To GOD belongs both the Hereafter, and this world. 

[53:26] Not even the angels in heaven possess authority to intercede. The 
only ones permitted by GOD are those who act in accordance with His will 
and His approval. 

--end quoted text

Now as I read this... the Prophet speaks of this vision and says to 
'compare this with the female idols... what a disgraceful distribution!', 
followed by the denunciation at 53:23 of 'These are but names you have 
made up... GOD never authorized such a blasphemy.  They follow conjecture, 
and personal desire, when the true guidance has come to them herein from 
their Lord.'

This, to my eyes, speaks of the 'female idols' *as opposed to* the 
Prophet's deity.

>
>and:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>Omniscient, Most Wise.

... again, followed by '53. He thus sets up the devil's scheme as a test 
for those who harbor doubts in their hearts, and those whose hearts are 
hardened. The wicked must remain with the opposition.'

>
>I will admit that I do not place the same emphasis upon IDOL as does
>the author of the criticism, but then I am not an Islamic scholar.

I will admit that I usually place less emphasis on the commentary and more 
on the text and context... and those draw me to an entirely different 
conclusion.

DD
```

_(thread depth: 34)_

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybubNOSPAM@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2007-05-15T20:01:10-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<134kluhisf83a53@news.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<bklp235d03auknn51jp1rt477h2fhmk6dc@4ax.com> <1178651948.257484.39320@y5g2000hsa.googlegroups.com> <f1s3g7$s30$1@reader2.panix.com> <1178736612.417596.27430@y80g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <f1t5tu$8sm$1@reader2.panix.com> <1179257635.289425.186730@k79g2000hse.googlegroups.com>`

```
Alistair wrote:
>
> As a rhetorical aside: The Koran that you refered to mentions, in one
> of the annotations, that M travelled at millions of times the speed of
> light. Wouldn't that have resulted in the universe becoming invisible?

Same problem as the inerrancy devotees. There are at least three problems 
with inerrancy:

1. When the text quotes someone, the text could be perfect but the speaker 
could be wrong (or lying).

2. The speaker could be exaggerating ("Solomon had treasures without end").

3. The translator or copyists could be wrong. The best example is the 
reverse of Occam's Razor: In Biblical translations, the more complex the 
text, the closer it is to being true. Often translators or copyists 
"simplified" the text to make it more palatable or understandable.

The basic rule is that it is not important what the Bible (or other sacred 
text) says. It is not even important what God (or other deity) says. What is 
important is what God meant! And often God did not say what He meant nor 
mean what He said.
```

>>

>I think that the Idols were thought to be deities too.

Exactly, Mr Maclean... 'thought to be' is not the same as 'being'.  53:21 
is, as I read it, a rhetorical question, asking if his Lord has such (as 
the idols) as daughters in the same manner that the reader/listener has 
sons; 53:22 - 3, as I read them, are repudiations of such a thought.

>There appears
>to be some notion where the idols were accorded the status of angels
>and that M is now backtracking.

Given the context I read it as the Prophet is denying 'the female idols' 
any kind of divinity whatsoever, either directly (in that they are divine) 
or by descent (in that they are daughters.

>More reading is in order, especially
>as the Vexen source seems, to my mind, to be less than impartial.

You noticed that, too?  I like to read the text and then the 
commentaries... then again, perhaps I am partial to my own impartialities.

[snip]

>> >I will admit that I do not place the same emphasis upon IDOL as does
>> >the author of the criticism, but then I am not an Islamic scholar.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>light. Wouldn't that have resulted in the universe becoming invisible?

Only according to some scientific standards, Mr Maclean... and religion is 
not, in my experience, science.

DD

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Military Ranks/Computers : WAS Re: newbie question on cobol syntax

_(thread depth: 32)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2007-05-09T09:37:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f1s4p2$30$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<bklp235d03auknn51jp1rt477h2fhmk6dc@4ax.com> <haSdnUo1i6lhRKLbnZ2dnUVZ_gydnZ2d@comcast.com> <f711435v2r7aflq1ofc1rgct3ro112k8eg@4ax.com> <5acfejF2o3ougU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
In article <5acfejF2o3ougU1@mid.individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
>"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
>news:f711435v2r7aflq1ofc1rgct3ro112k8eg@4ax.com...

[snip]

>> Humans are more driven by emotions than rationality.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>something is to recognise it.

'... is *now* (emphasis added) recognised'?  Hang on a moment, let me use 
a few words and a search engine to make myself look wiser than I am... 
ahhhh, there you are.  From 
ftp://opensource.nchc.org.tw/gutenberg/etext99/phdrs10.txt :

--begin quoted text:

Of the nature of the soul, though her true form be ever a theme of large 
and more than mortal discourse, let me speak briefly, and in a figure.  
And let the figure be composite--a pair of winged horses and a charioteer.

[snip]

...the human charioteer drives his in a pair; and one of them is noble and 
of noble breed, and the other is ignoble and of ignoble breed; and the 
driving of them of necessity gives a great deal of trouble to him.

[snip]

As I said at the beginning of this tale, I divided each soul into three--
two horses and a charioteer; and one of the horses was good and the other
bad:

--end quoted text

(pardon my midsentence snippage; I believe that the author's intent was 
maintained - a description of a charioteer driving two horses, one good 
and the other bad - but a glance at the text will show some weighty 
sentences, indeed)

So... it gets even better, Mr Dashwood; about two-and-a-half millennia 
back not only was the disjunction between intellect (charioteer) and 
emotions (horses) noticed but the difficulties that the intellect has 
controlling and balancing the good emotions (the right-hand horse) and the 
base emotions (the left-hand horse) rather prettily described.

Oh... and forget that Nietszche fellow who commented 'Whoever said 'Alas, 
two souls dwell within my breast!' fell short... by a goodly number of 
souls.'

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Military Ranks/Computers : WAS Re: newbie question on cobol syntax

_(thread depth: 33)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-05-10T00:36:16+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5adti2F2n8175U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<bklp235d03auknn51jp1rt477h2fhmk6dc@4ax.com> <haSdnUo1i6lhRKLbnZ2dnUVZ_gydnZ2d@comcast.com> <f711435v2r7aflq1ofc1rgct3ro112k8eg@4ax.com> <5acfejF2o3ougU1@mid.individual.net> <f1s4p2$30$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:f1s4p2$30$1@reader2.panix.com...
> In article <5acfejF2o3ougU1@mid.individual.net>,
> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[58 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, Doc, I have read Bhagavad Gita (indeed, the entire Mahabharata) where 
similar analogy was made around 2000 years ago... Krishna seems to have been 
a fairly capable charioteer; Arjuna's chariot had 4 horses if I remember 
correctly...Coping with these in the midst of battle was no mean feat.

I guess I should have qualified what I wrote.

Here, I'll do it now...

"At least the fact that we are emotional and, therefore, sometimes
irrational creatures, is now MORE GENERALLY recognised.  The first step 
towards dealing with
something is to recognise it."

My point was that a rise in "education" has raised the general awareness of 
our imperfections. I did not mean to imply that no philosopers had ever 
questioned this before.

Pete.
```

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()

In article <5adti2F2n8175U1@mid.individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:f1s4p2$30$1@reader2.panix.com...
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>> ahhhh, there you are.

[snip]

>> So... it gets even better, Mr Dashwood; about two-and-a-half millennia
>> back not only was the disjunction between intellect (charioteer) and
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>something is to recognise it."

Wow, now there's a neat trick... when a quantifiable specific is 
contradicted by documentation it is time to... change it to an 
unquantifiable generalisation!

('Oh, there's evidence to show 'is now recognised' is incorrect?  Change 
it to 'is MORE GENERALLY recognised', that'll take care of things.')

>
>My point was that a rise in "education" has raised the general awareness of 
>our imperfections. I did not mean to imply that no philosopers had ever 
>questioned this before.

My point was that lo, there is nothing completely new under the sun... but 
I think that's been said before.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Military Ranks/Computers : WAS Re: newbie question on cobol syntax

_(thread depth: 35)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-05-10T11:00:33+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5af24hF2ocetoU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<bklp235d03auknn51jp1rt477h2fhmk6dc@4ax.com> <5acfejF2o3ougU1@mid.individual.net> <f1s4p2$30$1@reader2.panix.com> <5adti2F2n8175U1@mid.individual.net> <f1si1o$nc6$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:f1si1o$nc6$1@reader2.panix.com...
> In article <5adti2F2n8175U1@mid.individual.net>,
> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[58 more quoted lines elided]…
> unquantifiable generalisation!

Watch and learn... :-)

>
> ('Oh, there's evidence to show 'is now recognised' is incorrect?  Change
> it to 'is MORE GENERALLY recognised', that'll take care of things.')
>

'is now recognised' is NOT incorrect. It is axiomatic... look around.

However, the inference drawn was not what I intended (I never intended to 
suggest that NO-ONE had ever recognised the imperfections of Humanity 
before; rather, that Behavioural sciences and the recognition and attempts 
to understand such imperfection is a comparatively recent phenomenon, and, 
with the increase in literacy and education over the last several thousand 
years, people are now more aware of their complexity than was previously the 
case.)

A perfectly reasonable and innocuous statement, I would've thought... 
(unless, of course, one is simply looking for an argument at any price)

At the time of the Sanskrit literature (even after the original Pali texts, 
which some suggest may be the oldest written record on the planet), people 
(most of whom could not read and write and so had not come into contact with 
philosophical wisdom except by listening to their priests) did not generally 
think about emotionality and rationality. Rather, whatever happened, 
(including the behaviour of Humans) was the Will of God (or Gods)

My statement simply suggests we have moved on from there.

>>
>
> My point was that lo, there is nothing completely new under the sun... but
> I think that's been said before.

I don't disagree. (Both with the statement and with its having been said 
before... :-))

Pete.
```

Howard Brazee wrote:
>
> Some of the economic theories that Republicans had about how to help
> the poor in the country made a lot of sense to me.    But once they
> got power, they didn't do anything to help small business, they were
> too beholden to big business.

Huh? Three tax cuts in three years, including a dramatic reduction in the 
death tax? Holding the line on minimum wage? Putting a stop to the welfare 
teat helped small business by forcing people into the job market. Health 
Savings Accounts and reduced regulation are also Republican initiatives. 
Very many "big businesses" started out in recent times as "small businesses" 
(Google, Ebay, Microsoft, Yahoo, Enron, YouTube, well, maybe not Enron).

> Rural towns voted Republican, but they are dying nevertheless.

As did Houston and other major cities in the South and West - but they're 
not dying.

>
> I haven't found that the Republicans in power have been big on fact.
> Certainly the poster boy Iraq war intelligence indicates not.

Uh, the intelligence on the Iraq war build up was orchestrated by a 
Democratic appointee (George Tenent).

>
> The Republicans certainly have big issues that are emotional in
> nature.

Um, that's certainly true if you equate religious belief with emotion.

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1178542172.049258.298830@h2g2000hsg.googlegroups.com...
> On 6 May, 14:08, "Pete Dashwood" <dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
> wrote:
…[65 more quoted lines elided]…
> That sounds like an interesting story. TELL ME.

Sorry, I have no intention of elaborating... (I don't come out of it very 
well if I did... and it was a long time ago) For me the important part was 
that (rather than being scared, which would have been a much more rational 
emotion), all I felt was anger (which is very rare for me). I can understand 
people who KNOW me wanting to shoot me :-), but total strangers? That's 
cruel and unnatural punishment...

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Military Ranks/Computers : WAS Re: newbie question on cobol syntax

_(thread depth: 27)_

- **From:** Michael Russell <Michael.Russell@msn.com>
- **Date:** 2007-05-10T18:02:26+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ibadnW5PKPGCzd7bnZ2dnUVZ8qSnnZ2d@pipex.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<5a0uirF2muhfjU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<bklp235d03auknn51jp1rt477h2fhmk6dc@4ax.com> <59snn9F2mag88U1@mid.individual.net> <1178214899.781963.175340@q75g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <59vkpgF2mphveU1@mid.individual.net> <f1evf0$g1t$1@reader2.panix.com> <5a0uirF2muhfjU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> 
> Democracy has to work, because all the alternatives are not even options, 
> for free people.
> 
> Pete.

It would be interesting to hear what 'democracy' and 'free' mean.

As far as I can tell, democracy means being able to vote for 
any of the candidates put before you .... by the oligarchy.

As for free - what comes first, civil freedom or economic freedom?
How do we see freedom 'in action', please?

It's a nice notion and kind of has an axiomatic ring - 
democracy has to work for free people - but I think it's a 
myth, so I don't subscribe.

If you subscribe to the idea, do please elucidate these 
fundamentals.

Michael
```

discussed in a Wall Street Journal I read in the 1980's or early
1990's shows a not so good side of the US.

- **References:** `<5984nrF2jrmikU1@mid.individual.net> <xpKdnV3cTJn0nq3bnZ2dnUVZ_vKunZ2d@comcast.com> <Js9Yh.131155$DE1.38614@pd7urf2no> <FfSdncXcZZry_6zbnZ2dnUVZ_h2pnZ2d@comcast.com> <CrgYh.132963$DE1.61323@pd7urf2no> <6eadnVLlIfQDA6_bnZ2dnUVZ_sudnZ2d@comcast.com> <0uUYh.143628$aG1.81147@pd7urf3no> <k_idndX2V7Xbo6jbnZ2dnUVZ_jqdnZ2d@comcast.com> <1177954440.699544.303810@e65g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <GOadnXDqfvKWNqvbnZ2dnUVZ_tWhnZ2d@comcast.com> <3sle33tu89ashekkambbf0n76gqhp0rhsg@4ax.com>`

```
Clark F Morris wrote:
> 
> Then why did the US send Canadian citizens of Syrian birth to Syria
> instead of allowing them to continue on to Canada?  Was Syria going to
> be used to torture information out of them why the US issued pious
> denials?  The hypocrisy in this case stinks.

I'm not familiar with that case - though, if you have a good place to 
read up about it, I'll put it in the "to read" queue.  Nevertheless, as 
an E-6 in the USAF, I don't have much say in immigration and visitation 
policies.  :)  If our country has messed up, I'll admit it - no one's 
perfect.

> I also believe that the
> Bush rules of engagement endanger US citizens everywhere by making
> renditions legitimate.

It depends on how wedded to terrorism some of these countries are.  If 
that's their way of life, US citizens should probably stay out of those 
countries.

You're never going to be 100% safe, though.  I'm sure you heard about 
the shooting at Virginia Tech a few weeks ago.  When someone is intent 
on committing evil, and is willing to die in that commission, it's 
impossible to completely stop.  You can take safeguards, and if you make 
yourself a hard target, you'll probably be OK.  (This assertion is 
backed up by real-life information from US citizens stationed abroad.)
```

Charles Hottel wrote:

>

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> There are programs now that are good at picking out the locations of voices.

Just so that we are all up to date: this week has seen the
announcement that a multi-processor IBM supercomputer has successfully
simulated roughly HALF of a MOUSE BRAIN. That is, approximately 6000
neurons out of 8000 on one side (it didn't say which side). So that is
6000 out of 16000 neurons. We have a long way to go to develop real
artificial intelligence.

I have posted this here because I'm too lazy to page back up and find
the start of this part of the thread.
```

- **References:** `<MhATh.2380$3P3.811@newsread3.news.pas.earthlink.net> <3SBTh.44480$EJ6.43955@newsfe24.lga> <_mUTh.2691$3P3.1491@newsread3.news.pas.earthlink.net> <bEOUh.10432$iq5.280939@wagner.videotron.net> <132eu1ma5tt65a0@news.supernews.com> <2o6g23tjhc1mkkmssbjtn2omp69q05aooj@4ax.com> <76th23lvh0gtgqpji35djej5t5dqbivhrp@4ax.com> <kgtZh.5567$Ut6.3966@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <59nu7tF2m9kmvU1@mid.individual.net> <4rle33lk61jrj2i6jbn7n837e8ia49trot@4ax.com>`

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:4rle33lk61jrj2i6jbn7n837e8ia49trot@4ax.com...
> On Tue, 1 May 2007 16:33:01 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> program a supercomputer to play chess than to walk.

Thanks Howard. My computer beats me at chess almost invariably. It's really 
depressing...I think I have an excellent campaign and it destroys me 
totally. Sometimes I marvel at how it could possibly have foreseen some 
outcomes and on the VERY rare occasions when I force a stalemate or actually 
win, I get the distinct feeling it LET me do so so I won't stop playing... 
:-)

The program is open source and was written at MIT... it plays up to 12 moves 
ahead; the best I can manage is  around 4. I can limit it to a certain 
"depth" and do so occasionally, but I always feel like I'm "cheating" if I 
do that (and I still lose... :-)).

From now on, whenever it gives me a hiding, I'll console myself with the 
trhought that: "OK, you might be good at chess, but you can't bloody well 
walk...!!!"


> Mice and birds
> have tremendously capable little brains that do much more than
> "think".

I wonder...    What kind of dreams and aspirations would they have?  Or 
maybe just staying alive requires the full capacity of their little brains.

Perhaps if Humans had never had leisure time we would not have come up with 
Art, Religion, Philosophy, Mathematics, etc.

Pete.
```

Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message >
> AI in general show up how little we know.    It is easier to program
> in "advanced functions" than primitive functions.    It's easier to
> program a supercomputer to play chess than to walk.   Mice and birds
> have tremendously capable little brains that do much more than
> "think".

Ants are even smarter, it seems.  I read in "Discover" (I think) that they
have a trigonometric calculator in their 40,000-cell brain that keeps
precise track of how far away from their parent "hill" they are, in
ant-steps, regardless of which direction or how far or how fast they travel:
apparently if you wait until they start to return, then cut their legs off
to half-length, they'll go exactly half-way back; or if you glue little
leg-extenders on to double the length they'll go just so far past their
hill.  I'm just reporting what I read!

PL
```

principals  looked like something from a TV comedy.

- **References:** `<MhATh.2380$3P3.811@newsread3.news.pas.earthlink.net> <3SBTh.44480$EJ6.43955@newsfe24.lga> <_mUTh.2691$3P3.1491@newsread3.news.pas.earthlink.net> <bEOUh.10432$iq5.280939@wagner.videotron.net> <132eu1ma5tt65a0@news.supernews.com> <2o6g23tjhc1mkkmssbjtn2omp69q05aooj@4ax.com> <76th23lvh0gtgqpji35djej5t5dqbivhrp@4ax.com> <kgtZh.5567$Ut6.3966@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <1178135233.415627.275200@e65g2000hsc.googlegroups.com>`

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1178135233.415627.275200@e65g2000hsc.googlegroups.com...
>
> Charles Hottel wrote:
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yeah and I think it took 10 seconds to simulate 1 second of mouse 
"thinking".

To Pete:

Thanks for the explanation of your joke. Explaining it did not spoil the 
joke as I had not a clue until I read the explanation. Actually I thought 
you were being serious.

Yes they have a book about health: "Fantastic Voyage: Live Long Enough To 
Live Forever".  It is not the supplements that will make you live forever it 
is the Sigularity and event leading up to it. From what I have read taking 
supplements may  help avoid or postpone some diseases but I do not think at 
this point there is any evidence of life extension.  The only way I know of 
extending maxmum life span is calorie restriction while optimizing nutrition 
(CRON).  That has worked in animal experiments and may work in humans, but I 
don't think you can consider it as scientifically proven.  Primates have 
long life spans and there has not been enough time for a full experiment. 
See "The 120 Year Diet" by Roy Walford. As he puts it in Chapter six page 
143: Where is the 45-Month-Old-Mouse? His diet, the CRON diet, extended 
mouse life span from38 months (human equivalent 110 years) to56 months 
(human equivalent 167 years). He has not seen even a 45 month mouse produced 
by any supplement, combination of supplements, or any hormone replacemnt 
therapy. That book was published in the year 2000. I have tried the diet and 
it quite hard to stick to it. He has some software to help you design you 
own recipes. I think he died in 2004 of ALS (Low Greigs disease).

Kurzweil also has an earlier book "The 10% Solution" which has diet and 
nutrition advice and tells hoe he beat type-ii diabetes and so far has 
postponed inherited tendencies for early heart disease.  I have not 
purchased any supplements from Kurzweil. I get most of min from ww.lef.org 
and they have a free online version of there book: Disease Treatment and 
Prevention that gives supplement recommendations for various diseases and 
conditions. Last year I was diagnosed with type-ii diabetes, high blood 
pressure and high cholesterol. By losing 55 pounds and taking some 
supplement they recommended my diabetes is completly gone and all my blood 
test numbers are now in normal range and my blood pressure is normal. For 
example my cholesterol dropped from 260 to 180. This is quite an 
accomplishment I think as I am still 90 pounds or more overweight.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The Future

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-05-03T12:43:55+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<59spicF2ln766U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<MhATh.2380$3P3.811@newsread3.news.pas.earthlink.net> <3SBTh.44480$EJ6.43955@newsfe24.lga> <_mUTh.2691$3P3.1491@newsread3.news.pas.earthlink.net> <bEOUh.10432$iq5.280939@wagner.videotron.net> <132eu1ma5tt65a0@news.supernews.com> <2o6g23tjhc1mkkmssbjtn2omp69q05aooj@4ax.com> <76th23lvh0gtgqpji35djej5t5dqbivhrp@4ax.com> <kgtZh.5567$Ut6.3966@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <1178135233.415627.275200@e65g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <mR8_h.3008$296.1145@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```

"Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote in message 
news:mR8_h.3008$296.1145@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net...
>
> "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
…[78 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Thanks Charlie, and very well done with your weight loss.

Your post made me realize that getting worried about a couple of kilos is a 
"nice" problem to have... Over the last 6 months I managed to lose 8 kilos 
without doing anything too radical (I really enjoy good food and wine and 
consider them an essential contribution to the quality of my life.) These 
days, if I see the trend going upward again I intervene. I hover around 100 
kilos (220 pounds) but this is OK as I am 6 foot and I feel fine. I have no 
illnesses and haven't had a cold for at least 10 years. (I'm just starting 
to get the first twinges of arthritis in the joints of one finger on my 
right hand...as long as I can still play guitar, I don't care :-))  I don't 
get nearly enough exercise, but I swim when I can and I walk around the 
Mount (about 2 miles) at least once a week. (That's mainly for smental 
refreshment, rather than corporeal :-)) Get a regular check up once a year 
and it is normal. I have taken vitamin supplements for over 30 years. Maybe 
it's psychological, but you won't hear me complaining :-)

Our bodies are complex, but selling snake oil is an age old tradition and 
nowadays it simply uses more modern technology. I don't believe there is any 
Elixir that will give us eternal youth (and I'm not sure I'd take it, if 
there was...) The whole pattern of our lives, our attitudes, interactions, 
food, drink, activity, enjoyment, all of these things reflect in our general 
health. I had a friend once who used to say that happy people don't get 
sick.  (he died at 89 years of age and his funeral was a hoot :-)) I think 
that's a bit unfair to the people who suffer ill health through no fault of 
their own, but I also think there are elements of truth in it. Maybe our 
immune systems react favourably to laughter. I hope so.

Pete.
```

<a id="t-8e46b386cd"></a>
## Thread: Calling programs on a UNIX server from Mainframe — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Calling programs on a UNIX server from Mainframe

- **From:** Catch_22 <catch_20_2@yahoo.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2007-05-01T21:59:49-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1178081989.668240.317210@y80g2000hsf.googlegroups.com>`

```
Hi,

We're running IBM Enterprise COBOL for z/OS  3.3.1.

We are looking at various software solutions to assist with cleansing
of data files. This cleansing process will need to be performed on a
daily basis as we receive new data from various sources. Unfortunately
most of the cleansing solutions we are looking at will need to run
either on a Windows or UNIX server (and are coded in either C++ or
Pearl).

This cleansing is essential to the quality of data being stored in our
mainframe DB though at the same time the actual nightly process is
extremely important to our business and cannot afford to be
interrupted/delayed.

If we do head down this path we will need to  :

(a). Transfer a file of data from the mainframe to the server on which
the cleansing is to occur. Trigger the software to cleanse the data
and create an output file. The transfer the data back to the
mainframe. (or try to transfer the data to the server first)

OR (b). Call the cleansing software from the mainframe, pass the data,
and receive the cleansed information back.

OR (c). Pass the data to a web service - have the web service perform
the cleansing by calling the software - return the data back to the
COBOL program.

I'm trying to evaluate :
(a) what options we have
(b) what sort of effort these options will take to implement

I have been looking through as many sources as possible to try and get
a feel for people's past experience with this kind of scenario and
cannot find a great deal. I have heard of products such as IBM
Websphere etc.

Has anyone here got any advice on what options I have or can point me
toward some information that will help us determine the best way
forward ?

One key aspect is we are looking at moving off the mainframe in the
next 2-3 years so we would like development effort and costs to be
reasonable ... this is not a long term investment.

Thanks in advance.
```

#### ↳ Re: Calling programs on a UNIX server from Mainframe

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-05-03T01:45:45+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<59rj09F2mjl8qU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1178081989.668240.317210@y80g2000hsf.googlegroups.com>`

```

"Catch_22" <catch_20_2@yahoo.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1178081989.668240.317210@y80g2000hsf.googlegroups.com...
> Hi,
>
…[46 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The following is opinion based on what you posted; it is free advice and 
should be treated as such. If you want details and references, examples, and 
consultancy, I charge for that.

1. Web Services are definitely the way to go.

2. I am just completing an exercise that uses a Web Service to do something 
very similar to what you describe. The only difference is that in my case 
they are web clients who are accessing the service over the Internet. In 
yours, it will be a mainframe and the service will run over your intranet.

I opted for a Web Service after discussing implications with people I 
respect and whose judgement I value. I wasn't keen on the idea but I have to 
say, I am really glad I opted for this. The more I have learned about it, 
the more persuaded I have become.

I'm now a Believer... Web Services rule!

3. The advantage of a Web Service is that it is totally platform independent 
and can run anywhere you need it. It is invokable from any language on any 
platform that can access the network, including COBOL. It is virtually 
"future proof"; as long as that service is required, it runs, and anything 
with access to the network can use it.

4. There are no hassles with updating client software; everything is in one 
place and doesn't need to be duplicated, as it is accessible from anywhere 
any time.

5. Look at Websphere to access a Web Service directly from your mainframe. 
However, that is not the ONLY way.

(In C# it is less than 20 lines of code to provide a web service; the 
service is really a "wrapper" to present a consistent interface. Most 
services use SOAP as the transport protocol and it is very effective.) 
Calling it from COBOL is a doddle; calling it from a CICS transaction is 
also trivial. You can also use mainframe COBOL to WRITE your web service, 
but this involves interfacing to the SOAP server and is a bit tiresome 
because the tools are not there.... (under VS 2005 this SOAP interface is 
completely generated for you; C# does the WSDL as well...).

A CAUTION: I'm not sure about SOAP services under z/OS; I know it all works 
under VSE. Bill could probably comment.

General considerations:

1. You need to protect your overnight batch window, so you don't want 
cleansing running at the end of the day.

2. But, presumably, the data should be cleansed before batch updating 
occurs.

Running a web service allows the various sources to be cleansed as they are 
received. It can be transactional or batch, and it can run as background or 
foreground with appropriate priorities. As data arrives, it is presented to 
the service, and then staged for overnight update.

You don't state volumes, but given they are not outrageous, there should be 
little or no discernible impact on the system if you do transactional 
cleansing.

The concept is to create a data "bathroom" that is available 24/7. Incoming 
data is routed to it, then clean data is applied to the back end updates 
overnight, or even online after cleansing. The rules for cleansing and the 
cross referencing, sorting, and parsing, are all part of the web service. 
The design of the bathroom should be to detect the type of incoming data and 
apply the correct rule sets to it. It is like a process machine on a 
production line.

From the mainframe programmer's POV it is simply passing data to a service, 
then doing something with it when it comes back, secure in the knowledge it 
is clean and valid.

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Calling programs on a UNIX server from Mainframe

- **From:** Catch_22 <catch_20_2@yahoo.co.uk>

```
Hi Pete,

Thank you for such a comprehensive response. I'm really appreciative
of the detail that you've gone into.
I can clarify some of the outstanding issues .....

Yes it is all nightly processing and I agree that processing the
addresses during the day rather than doing it in one big bang at the
end of the day creates less risk for the nightly processing should
there be server issues etc. The volume will not be huge quantities
though at peak periods we may well be talking in the thousands -
though no more than 10,000.

The issue I will run into is lack of experience within the company on
how to set this up.
Our mainframe is overseas & outsourced and I don't think they have the
technical ability to set up the infrastructure to have this operating
smoothly.

Currently if we don't run the web service we would need to switch some
of the logic in our CICS screens that use this functionality as we
won't be able to validate the data in real time - the web service
would allow us to do this. This is why I was asking for any
suggestions on whether there was anywhere that had an overview about
how such a system could be set up. Switching off the logic in the CICS
screens will cause the business some discomfort till we move off the
mainframe.

As usual time constraints on this project are pretty tight and the
business has sat on this project for a long time and now want it
implemented ASAP ..... I'm trying to look at ways of avoiding the
inconvenience of moving to a batch solution which will certainly
result in a drop in efficiency and certainly cause a great deal of
angst in the business.

At risk of stretching the friendship, any suggestions you might be
able to provide would be appreciated. :-)
I would say that there would be no other alternative at this stage
other than to get a consultant in with the experinence.

Regards,
Ian
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Calling programs on a UNIX server from Mainframe

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-05-04T13:47:30+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<59vhlkF2msvgcU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1178081989.668240.317210@y80g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <59rj09F2mjl8qU1@mid.individual.net> <1178236420.423588.265380@l77g2000hsb.googlegroups.com>`

```

"Catch_22" <catch_20_2@yahoo.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1178236420.423588.265380@l77g2000hsb.googlegroups.com...
> Hi Pete,
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> though no more than 10,000.

That should be no problem at all with a transactional web service approach, 
given you have the processing power available on your UNIX box, and 
reasonable bandwith on your intranet.

>
> The issue I will run into is lack of experience within the company on
> how to set this up.

Presumably your Unix guys can deliver the Web Service. (It is really just a 
matter of exposing existing Methods to SOAP).

You don't say what they are writing the data cleansing in, but Java or C++ 
both contain web service support classes, and if they are using Dot NET, the 
FCL has superb support for them. (I use C# to access these Classes and it is 
competely possible to build a web service in less than half an hour, once 
you have done a few and know your way around.) I don't think your problem 
will be here; it is more likely to be a problem for the COBOL guys on the 
mainframe, who are unfamiliar with Object Orientation, don't understand what 
Methods are, and will have some trepidation about going outside their 
comfort zone. This is normal, and can be addressed by presenting the 
concepts to them and providing sample code that works.


> Our mainframe is overseas & outsourced and I don't think they have the
> technical ability to set up the infrastructure to have this operating
> smoothly.

You may be underestimating them. It really isn't complex. If they can run a 
mainframe they can certainly set up a SOAP server on it.

>
> Currently if we don't run the web service we would need to switch some
> of the logic in our CICS screens that use this functionality as we
> won't be able to validate the data in real time - the web service
> would allow us to do this.

You should set some design criteria and one of them should be: "Minimum 
impact on our existing systems"

Don't change the CICS stuff unless you REALLY have to, and you shouldn't...

The Web Service approach avoids this, as you realized yourself.


>This is why I was asking for any
> suggestions on whether there was anywhere that had an overview about
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> implemented ASAP .....

Funny... I've never encountered that scenarion before... :-)

>I'm trying to look at ways of avoiding the
> inconvenience of moving to a batch solution which will certainly
> result in a drop in efficiency and certainly cause a great deal of
> angst in the business.

Yes, you are right. Batch processing is best avoided. (Sometimes you CAN'T 
avoid it, and we had a debate about that here a few months back. I believe 
it can be totally eradicated, (certainly as far as new systems are 
concerned) but others disagree. Anyway, in the real world where you are 
living at the moment, you are wise not to move towards it if you don't 
really have to.)
>
> At risk of stretching the friendship, any suggestions you might be
> able to provide would be appreciated. :-)

I'm really happy to help, but I can't do much more than I have already 
without a lot more information. It seems to me you are on the right track.

> I would say that there would be no other alternative at this stage
> other than to get a consultant in with the experinence.

That is probably the best approach. Ensure you find someone who has written 
COBOL code on a mainframe (possibly out of a CICS transaction) to access a 
web service. The CV should have "SOAP" on it ...and the applicant should 
know what it stands for :-)

(The above is NOT an endorsement for generally selecting applicants based on 
keywords in their CV; it is simply ensuring that, in this particular case, 
an absolute requirement is met)

Where are you located? If it's somewhere nice I might take a holiday :-)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Calling programs on a UNIX server from Mainframe

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-05-04T15:50:59+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<59vot4F2mi195U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1178081989.668240.317210@y80g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <59rj09F2mjl8qU1@mid.individual.net> <1178236420.423588.265380@l77g2000hsb.googlegroups.com> <59vhlkF2msvgcU1@mid.individual.net> <1178245258.897405.120000@n59g2000hsh.googlegroups.com>`

```

"Catch_22" <catch_20_2@yahoo.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1178245258.897405.120000@n59g2000hsh.googlegroups.com...
> Ha ha thanks again .... we're located in Sydney though have access to
> some other global centres so we're going to start scouring our
> outposts to see if any of them have done any of this work before.

I was in Sydney a couple of weeks back, visiting friends.

If I'd known, we could've had lunch.

>
> Our business has a strong .Net team and mainframe programmers who are
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> system.

Hmmm... without seeing how it failed I can't comment.

Web Services 101:

1. A web service is a Class that runs on a server and exposes various 
methods to the world. (The "world" is the visible network.)

2. Each user of the service instantiates their own copy of it as is usual in 
OO environments. (Clients see it as an object).

3. The required parameters and interfaces to the service are described in 
WSDL (Web Services Definition Language). This is so akin to XML that you 
could be forgiven for not seeing any diference :-))

4. Once we know what the service receives and transmits (from the WSDL) we 
simply need to transport that data from and to our invoking program. This is 
accomplished by "wrapping" the parameters (and the response) in XML and 
transporting this "wrapped XML" across the network using a protocol called 
SOAP. (In its infancy, when I first started using it, this stood for "Simple 
Object Access Protocol" which is pretty self-explanatory and has a 
comforting word "simple" in it...:-) Unfortunately, SOAP has moved on and is 
no longer simple, so the acronym, while it still represents the protocol, is 
no longer official and has been "de-registered"... SOAP doesn't stand for 
anything any more, it just represents a protocol...)

Given the information above, you can see that in order to communicate with 
your web service you must create XML in your COBOL program, that wraps the 
data the service needs to process. You can accomplish this pretty easily by 
using a template for the message.
>
> At either end of the system COBOL and .Net I believe we'll be able to
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Good stuff... you are finding what is needed. Don't be intimidated by XML; 
it is just like HTML inasmuch as it is text, with tags in it.

Seeing you're actually trying (so many who come here don't...:-)) I'll give 
you some more clues :-)

Here's some SOAP....

SOAP 1.1
The following is a sample SOAP 1.1 request. The PLACEHOLDERS shown need to 
be replaced with actual values.

POST /AVSWebService/AVSWebService.asmx HTTP/1.1
Host: primacomputing.co.nz
Content-Type: text/xml; charset=utf-8
Content-Length: LENGTH
SOAPAction: "http://primacomputing.co.nz/AVSWebService/ValidateNZaddress"

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<soap:Envelope xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance" 
xmlns:xsd="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema" 
xmlns:soap="http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/soap/envelope/">
  <soap:Body>
    <ValidateNZaddress xmlns="http://primacomputing.co.nz/AVSWebService/">
      <AVSInterfaceBlock>STRING</AVSInterfaceBlock>
    </ValidateNZaddress>
  </soap:Body>
</soap:Envelope>Ths is describing the interface to a web service called 
"AVSWebService" and invoking the method called "ValidateNZaddress". So this 
is what theCOBOL program needs to build in order to use the service.

Notice the tag: <AVSInterfaceBlock>... the word "STRING" is a placeholder 
and needs to be replaced with the
actual interface block (The LENGTH in Content-Length: would need to be set 
as well, if we were doing this from a Browser)

Build a text file (template)  using the second part of the above... like 
this...

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<soap:Envelope xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance" 
xmlns:xsd="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema" 
xmlns:soap="http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/soap/envelope/">
  <soap:Body>
    <ValidateNZaddress xmlns="http://primacomputing.co.nz/AVSWebService/">
      <AVSInterfaceBlock>STRING</AVSInterfaceBlock>
    </ValidateNZaddress>
  </soap:Body>
</soap:Envelope>

Read the file and write each line to an output .XML file. When you hit the 
STRING, replace it with the interface block you have previously constructed 
in your Working-Storage  and continue to EOF.

Close the output and make your SOAP call, referencing the XML you just 
created. Everything the system needs to know is in the XML (SOAP envelope). 
The service will be passed your interface block and the ValidateNZaddress 
method will be activated.

The results will arrive back as another XML file (or in-memory object).

Notice that the service can be located anywhere on Earth if you are using 
the internet (as we are here). You could actually invoke this service from 
Sydney and it would instantly return data to you. It is a PUBLIC WebService; 
anyone can use it.

(It won't, because I have not activated it yet...and I am still building the 
login for it.. But it has been tested thoroughly and works :-))

If you want to see the format of the returned XML just enter this link into 
your Browser:

http://primacomputing.co.nz/AVSWebService/AVSWebService.asmx

If you want to see the WSDL... :
http://primacomputing.co.nz/AVSWebService/AVSWebService.asmx?wsdl

Remember that, from the Client end you don't have to worry about the WSDL, 
it is only for defining services and VS 2005 generates it all anyway...


> The difficulty is in finding a solution in a short period of time that
> will meet the business needs and be able to be implemented in a short
> period of time ...... the usual story ...... want the world for
> minimum effort :-) I'm just hoping to find the middle ground.
>

I thnk you are well on the way :-)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Calling programs on a UNIX server from Mainframe

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2007-05-04T05:44:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SKz_h.165137$RF.29503@fe02.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<1178081989.668240.317210@y80g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <59rj09F2mjl8qU1@mid.individual.net> <1178236420.423588.265380@l77g2000hsb.googlegroups.com>`

```
Jumping into the dialog between you two ....

If I understand your situation, the current application is a CICS online 
application that includes "data validation" in "real time" during CICS 
transactions - but that you are LOOKING at moving to either

A) a "nightly" data validation (clean up) system
      or
B) using "web services" from your COBOL CICS application rather than the 
existing COBOL portion of the CICS application.

 If this isn't what you are asking about, then sorry for jumping in.

It would seem to me that "approach A" would be a non-starter.  It would require 
(I believe) substantive changes to the business application.  If, today, the 
system ASSUMES that data is validated when entered, that trying to "off-load" 
this to nightly or batch processing could cause serious problems in the "rest of 
the system" that is currently designed assuming "validation at time of data 
entry".

If you are looking at going to "approach B", then (for an existing IBM CICS 
application), I would bet that MQSeries would be the best and easiest way to 
"communicate" with the available (non-CICS, web-based) validation facilities. If 
this sounds reasonable and possible, I can get some references for you on how to 
integrate MQSeries into your existing COBOL/CICS applications.
```

### Cobol typical problem

- **From:** Suravee.Mishra@gmail.com
- **Date:** 2007-05-01T22:33:47-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Hi,

   Our database is ingres and for some programs we are using embeded
cobol programming. And I am facing a very typical problem.

   Just before the insert statement to tha database while i display,
it shows me correct result which is 121 crores but while the data is
inserted into the database only 21 crores is inserted.

The field size is defined as S9(12)V99.
What might be the problem??

Th
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol typical problem

- **From:** Louis Krupp <lkrupp@pssw.nospam.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 2007-05-02T07:57:07-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<133h661bvtcr2fa@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1178084027.240287.176670@p77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<1178084027.240287.176670@p77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com>`

```
Suravee.Mishra@gmail.com wrote:
> Hi,
> 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I have no idea what the problem is, but I did a Google search for 
"crore," and some things became clear:

An Indian crore is ten million according to Wikipedia and one other 
source.  (The word is not commonly used outside the Indian subcontinent 
and is probably not good to use when communicating with English speakers 
elsewhere.)

It sounds like a field with the value (in American terms)

1,210,000,000

is being truncated to

210,000,000

when it is stored in a database record.

The database record description (schema, or whatever Ingres calls it) is 
probably worth checking.

Louis
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol News - Microfocus and AcuCOBOL

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-05-08T01:48:29+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5a8p1fF2mq7k3U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1178289593.102426.302630@u30g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <pNS_h.104316$VU4.101008@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <MtCdnZhWqo6zoKHbnZ2dnUVZ8tSdnZ2d@pipex.net> <1oc%h.265046$c62.228514@fe07.news.easynews.com> <5a62r0F2mo062U1@mid.individual.net> <463e4cf9$0$8983$4c368faf@roadrunner.com>`

```

"Colin Campbell" <cmcampb@adelphia.net> wrote in message 
news:463e4cf9$0$8983$4c368faf@roadrunner.com...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Almost undoubtedly, YES!  <g>

OK, Colin, thanks for qualifying your response... :-)
>
> (The "obsolete language" part is a bit unkind.

No, it isn't. It is a simple statement of fact. (How much longer it will 
still be arguable remains to be seen;  I don't think we'll be having this 
discussion in 2015...)

I know people who still make lace by hand; that doesn't mean their process 
for doing so isn't obsolete.

Your old Company may well still be using COBOL, and maybe they'll never move 
to the Internet, in which case it doesn't matter too much whether they 
continue using COBOL. Eventually, if that is really the case, they'll either 
find themselves overrun by their competitors, or sales will drop to the 
point where the cost of continung COBOL committment is more than they can 
afford.

The Network has inherited the Earth; COBOL does not cope well with the 
network, the result is inevitable. (OO COBOL actually copes very well with 
the Network, but it can't compete with the other OO languages on price, 
toolsets, or infrastructure.C# and Java both leave it gasping, and they are 
free...)

10 - 15 years or so ago, if the COBOL community had embraced OO, the 
language might have had a chance of still being viable. They didn't, it 
isn't. Now it is far too late. The people who need to use OO (and that's 
everyone who intends to use the Net) won't use COBOL, or even OO COBOL, 
because they have infinitely better tools, IDEs, and languages to write 
stuff in, they are not hit with run time fees for what they develop, and 
they don't have to pay thousands of dollars for a compiler, or more 
thousands for training.

Simple Darwinism; fail to adapt, get replaced by a species that did.  COBOL 
is obsolete, in the same sense that Neanderthals were obsolete once Homo 
Sapiens arrived.

I'm not saying it to be unkind (I actually love COBOL, and wrote it for 
nearly 40 years... I'm just realistic enough to recognise the situation.) 
I'm currently converting OO COBOL components to C#, wrapping other COBOL 
components in C#, writing new stuff as web services in C#, and finding life 
is much easier.

>I know the organization I retired from is still writing new COBOL programs, 
>as well as maintaining thousands of existing ones.  Of course, 99.9% of the 
>code executes on an IBM mainframe....)

Sure. I wonder what percentage of it will do so by 2015 (my publicly 
predicted date for the end of COBOL as a serious development language)?

Pete
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol News - Microfocus and AcuCOBOL

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** adestrup@gmail.com
- **Date:** 2007-05-08T23:46:32-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1178693192.699748.101870@e65g2000hsc.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<5acemfF2oe62dU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1178289593.102426.302630@u30g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <pNS_h.104316$VU4.101008@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <MtCdnZhWqo6zoKHbnZ2dnUVZ8tSdnZ2d@pipex.net> <1oc%h.265046$c62.228514@fe07.news.easynews.com> <5a62r0F2mo062U1@mid.individual.net> <463e4cf9$0$8983$4c368faf@roadrunner.com> <5a8p1fF2mq7k3U1@mid.individual.net> <1178651347.798594.117310@u30g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <5acemfF2oe62dU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Do you really think that C# or VB.NET are the languages of the future?

Twenty years ago I read the same things about C, C++ and VB but that
didn't happen!

What I wrote twenty years ago using VB should be almost completely
rewritten in VB.NET, without any real advantage: nothing changes from
a user's point of view, apart  the need of a bigger machine, and a lot
of serious problems for the programmer.

What I wrote in COBOL twenty years ago can stay there with minor
changes, again: nothing changes from a user's point of view but, for
the programmer ... a big difference!

Apart the durability of your code there's another question which I
think shoud be considered: complexity and readability of code.
Considering a serious application (2.000 to 3.000 programs and hudreds
of thousand of code lines) I think that C#/VB/VB.NET readability and
complexity is far more difficult to maintain than COBOL.

COBOL is certainly very old in its structure; it's OO features, where
present, are not much appreciated and used by programmers; it's
graphical interface, where it exists, is not always easy to be
implemented; etc., etc.

That's all true! But I wouldn't prefer C# or VB.NET just because thier
features are easier to be implemented: what happens if MS decides to
come out with C## or VB#.NET? What about the readability of hunfreds
of thousand of code lines? What about a new ADO#.NET or things like
these?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol News - Microfocus and AcuCOBOL

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-05-09T23:38:19+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5adq5bF2oc19aU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1178289593.102426.302630@u30g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <pNS_h.104316$VU4.101008@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <MtCdnZhWqo6zoKHbnZ2dnUVZ8tSdnZ2d@pipex.net> <1oc%h.265046$c62.228514@fe07.news.easynews.com> <5a62r0F2mo062U1@mid.individual.net> <463e4cf9$0$8983$4c368faf@roadrunner.com> <5a8p1fF2mq7k3U1@mid.individual.net> <1178651347.798594.117310@u30g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <5acemfF2oe62dU1@mid.individual.net> <1178693192.699748.101870@e65g2000hsc.googlegroups.com>`

```

<adestrup@gmail.com> wrote in message 
news:1178693192.699748.101870@e65g2000hsc.googlegroups.com...
> Do you really think that C# or VB.NET are the languages of the future?
>

No, I think they are the languages of today.

It isn't about languages; it is about changing perceptions of data 
processing and how information is shared and utilised. It is about a 
different model and a different paradigm from procedural coding.

> Twenty years ago I read the same things about C, C++ and VB but that
> didn't happen!

Must have been wrong then... :-)


>
> What I wrote twenty years ago using VB should be almost completely
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

If nothing changes from a User's point of view then either your business is 
very static or your understanding of cyber technology is very narrow. Either 
way, your users are not being well served.

I disagree strongly about problems for the programmer. Today's tool set is 
infinitely better than yesterday's, the languages are more efficient and 
quicker and easier to write, it is almost impossible to code incorrectly 
thanks to features like Intellisense, and all you have to focus on is the 
logic you require. Modern software development is more like assembling LEGO 
than coding a line by line solution. How is that more problematic for the 
programmer?


> What I wrote in COBOL twenty years ago can stay there with minor
> changes, again: nothing changes from a user's point of view but, for
> the programmer ... a big difference!
>

Well, if the business you are in has remained static for twenty years, then 
I won't argue with you. What I will say is, it is exceptional. Most 
businesses need to adapt to an increasingly competitive and dynamic market 
place. Fundamental to this is the rapid exchange and sharing of information 
and that is why the Internet has changed the way we live and do business. 
Companies that can get by without being affected are probably in niche 
markets where there is not so much competition.

> Apart the durability of your code there's another question which I
> think shoud be considered: complexity and readability of code.
> Considering a serious application (2.000 to 3.000 programs and hudreds
> of thousand of code lines) I think that C#/VB/VB.NET readability and
> complexity is far more difficult to maintain than COBOL.

There is no need to maintain code at all if you opt for an object oriented, 
component-based approach. I've covered this in depth here and elsewhere, on 
other occasions. It is a hard concept for COBOL people to grasp.. Objects 
are encapsulated and re-used. They should NOT be maintained. That is one 
major difference between the OO and Procedural paradigms. It is too much to 
go into here, but if you are still thinking in terms of maintaining source 
code then there's little point me pursuing this.

COBOL was/is ideally suited to a procedural paradigm where source code and 
the ease of maintenance of it is everything. That is not the modern idiom. 
Some sites DO maintain Classes and and tinker constantly with their Java or 
whatever. I don't recommend this approach and I have seen it fail in several 
shops.

For my own part, I don't maintain the C# I write. It does what it does; new 
functionality comes along, it inherits what is there already and extends it. 
The existing source, once it is debugged and performing according to 
specification does not get changed. Internals of Class libraries do not get 
maintained on my watch.

>
> COBOL is certainly very old in its structure; it's OO features, where
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
No problem. Classes and objects remain the same. The 80,000 or so classes 
that constitute Dot NET remain the same. There may be some new ones, there 
may be some new interfaces, there may be some extensions that inherit 
previous methods, properties, and events, but you can bet that your code 
will continue to work. Not only in MicroSoft, but  on any other platforms 
that implement the FCL also. (I have C# code that has run on Linux, just to 
see if it would... :-))

First of all there aren't hundreds of thousands of lines of code (that you 
can/must manipulate), and even if there were, that code is invoking methods 
and propertiers of Classes that you don't write or maintain.  They are 
pieces of encapsulated functionality (functions, if you like) that you 
simply glue together. (or break apart and re-use). The days of sacred source 
code ruling everything are over. That was COBOL. Today, the object code 
embodied in a Class Library is what matters and you won't have source for it 
(unless it is your own library which you wrote yourself, of course), will 
never need to have source for it, and couldn't amend it if you wanted to. 
The actual source is of purely academic interest; the FUNCTIONALITY is 
everything. You don't worry about the source of your Operating System, yet 
you use the Classes and Objects embodied in it every day. You expect them to 
work, and they do. As specified. The same is true for the Dot NET Framework.

There is no comparison between traditional procedural COBOL and a modern OO 
language like C# or Java. They are different paradigms, meeting different 
criteria. You might as well compare oranges and submarines...

Pete.
```

Really Pete.

I'm a C++ programmer as well.... and coded some of sub-modules in C++,

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol News - Microfocus and AcuCOBOL

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-05-10T21:42:16+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5ag7noF2ohe2nU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1178289593.102426.302630@u30g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <pNS_h.104316$VU4.101008@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <MtCdnZhWqo6zoKHbnZ2dnUVZ8tSdnZ2d@pipex.net> <1oc%h.265046$c62.228514@fe07.news.easynews.com> <5a62r0F2mo062U1@mid.individual.net> <463e4cf9$0$8983$4c368faf@roadrunner.com> <5a8p1fF2mq7k3U1@mid.individual.net> <1178651347.798594.117310@u30g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <5acemfF2oe62dU1@mid.individual.net> <1178693192.699748.101870@e65g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <5adq5bF2oc19aU1@mid.individual.net> <1178767753.307905.221640@w5g2000hsg.googlegroups.com>`

```

"Rene_Surop" <infodynamics_ph@yahoo.com> wrote in message 
news:1178767753.307905.221640@w5g2000hsg.googlegroups.com...
> On May 9, 7:38 pm, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> business process change, apparently your code changes.

What I think is that you wrote COBOL before you wrote C++.

(This is not a criticism and is not meant to be disparaging).

> Even OO code
> for that matter because we have to add additional parameters to thos
> already coded OO application.

I don't; I use Getters and Setters and don't pass parameters, for the most 
part.  This means interfaces don't change.

The whole point of Object Orientation is to use objects.  Objects should be 
encapsulated. Mine are.

It is all very arguable and I'm sick of arguing it here, so I wont. :-) 
(there have been heated debates in the OO community about use of getters and 
setters; opinion is divided. I use what works for me, and I have not 
maintained code for about 5 years now, despite enhancing existing live 
systems.)

What I will say, is that if you code in an Object Oriented language and 
still maintain code, then you are not getting all of the benefits you could 
be from such an approach. All you have done is transfer an existing mind set 
to a new platform.

I reckon (based on 40 years experience around the world) that most of the 
errors in live systems are caused by maintaining them. If you push me I'd 
say at least 85%. Sometimes knock on errors are introduced by maintenance, 
that are so subtle they take considerabloe time to manifest themselves. 
Often changes are introduced under pressure and stress, without being 
thoroughly thought through and without being fully regression tested.  Often 
the person or persons making the change are not the people who wrote the 
original code and don't have the benefit of the background understanding 
that developers acquire by working for months/years on a system. When 
systems are developed they are designed and tested thoroughly, and, for the 
most part, on day one they work. It is when it needs "just this one small 
change" that the trouble starts...

Systems that don't require maintenance don't require regression testing 
either. Encapsulated functionality CANNOT be changed by the introduction of 
change elsewhere. Everything that used to work, still works, because it 
hasn't been changed. Only the new classes/methods/properties need to be 
tested.

A component based approach to system engineering permits this. You don't 
strip an engine down because a new starter motor was fitted.

Sure, I know it's heresy... (at least, it is to COBOL people) so burn me :-)

>
> Haven't you tried looking at the size (in Kb) of MSVCRT.DLL module? It
> did change from time to time.
>
A library size can change because new methods/properties/events are added. 
Anyway, I take no responsibility for what MicroSoft do with their software 
:-).

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol News - Microfocus and AcuCOBOL

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2007-05-10T05:03:02-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1178798582.549183.180370@q75g2000hsh.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<5ag7noF2ohe2nU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1178289593.102426.302630@u30g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <pNS_h.104316$VU4.101008@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <MtCdnZhWqo6zoKHbnZ2dnUVZ8tSdnZ2d@pipex.net> <1oc%h.265046$c62.228514@fe07.news.easynews.com> <5a62r0F2mo062U1@mid.individual.net> <463e4cf9$0$8983$4c368faf@roadrunner.com> <5a8p1fF2mq7k3U1@mid.individual.net> <1178651347.798594.117310@u30g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <5acemfF2oe62dU1@mid.individual.net> <1178693192.699748.101870@e65g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <5adq5bF2oc19aU1@mid.individual.net> <1178767753.307905.221640@w5g2000hsg.googlegroups.com> <5ag7noF2ohe2nU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On 10 May, 10:42, "Pete Dashwood" <dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
wrote:
> I reckon (based on 40 years experience around the world) that most of the
> errors in live systems are caused by maintaining them. If you push me I'd
> say at least 85%. Sometimes knock on errors are introduced by maintenance,
> that are so subtle they take considerabloe time to manifest themselves.

Personally, I'd say that 100% of errors are caused by users wanting
systems developed and/or changed. Seriously, though, in my experience,
the majority of errors that I have encountered were created at
development/enhancement time and only a small number of errors were
introduced by maintenance work post-implementation.

> Often changes are introduced under pressure and stress, without being
> thoroughly thought through and without being fully regression tested.

True, more often than not. Often, new developments are implemented
without being adequately tested or even without being tested at all (I
remember one developer who said that he ran out of budget and put the
system live untested and anyway, wasn't it the role of the support
team to be called out in the night to fix bugs?)

> Often
> the person or persons making the change are not the people who wrote the
> original code and don't have the benefit of the background understanding
> that developers acquire by working for months/years on a system.

More often than not I would say that those who enhance/maintain
systems are rarely the saps who wrote the rubbish in the first place
(I spent the best part of 7 years enhancing/maintaining systems on a
mainframe support team where I was the only original developer and it
was obvious how little the development team knew of the systems by the
fact that they came running to me every time they had to change a line
of code to make certain that they were doing the right thing.
Effectively spending my budget doing their job).

> When
> systems are developed they are designed and tested thoroughly,

BOLLOCKS! No offence intended just I wish to express succinctly how
much I disagree with that statement (I refer you to my comments
above).

> and, for the
> most part, on day one they work.

For the most part? So, for a significant part, they don't work upon
implementation? Isn't development a cloud cuckoo-land ivory tower if
it can not produce working code?

> It is when it needs "just this one small
> change" that the trouble starts...

It is true that enhancements and maintenance can introduce errors.
This is documented as a known fact in numerous places and is drummed
into all trainees (isn't it?). However, I doubt if the introduced
error rate for maintenance or enhancements is any worse than the error
rate for green-field developments.

>
> Systems that don't require maintenance don't require regression testing
> either.

Systems that don't require computer code to be designed/written/tested/
implemented don't require regression testing either. I presume that
you mean that your much vaunted  OO development source doesn't need
regression testing.

> Encapsulated functionality CANNOT be changed by the introduction of
> change elsewhere. Everything that used to work, still works, because it
> hasn't been changed. Only the new classes/methods/properties need to be
> tested.

What happens when developer A codes OO source to handle file A and
then developer B gets tasked with generalising the code to cater for
all file types, etc.?

>
> A component based approach to system engineering permits this. You don't
> strip an engine down because a new starter motor was fitted.

But you would replace the starter motor if it wasn't beefy enough to
turn the engine over.

>
> Sure, I know it's heresy... (at least, it is to COBOL people) so burn me :-)

A pleasure. When are you coming to the UK? Bugger! I just remembered
the witchcraft laws were repealed a little while ago.

>
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> :-).

Very wise, that would give us two reasons to burn you at the stake.
```

- **References:** `<1178289593.102426.302630@u30g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <pNS_h.104316$VU4.101008@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <MtCdnZhWqo6zoKHbnZ2dnUVZ8tSdnZ2d@pipex.net> <1oc%h.265046$c62.228514@fe07.news.easynews.com> <5a62r0F2mo062U1@mid.individual.net> <463e4cf9$0$8983$4c368faf@roadrunner.com> <5a8p1fF2mq7k3U1@mid.individual.net> <1178651347.798594.117310@u30g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <5acemfF2oe62dU1@mid.individual.net> <1178693192.699748.101870@e65g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <5adq5bF2oc19aU1@mid.individual.net> <1178767753.307905.221640@w5g2000hsg.googlegroups.com> <5ag7noF2ohe2nU1@mid.individual.net> <1178798582.549183.180370@q75g2000hsh.googlegroups.com>`

```
"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1178798582.549183.180370@q75g2000hsh.googlegroups.com...
> On 10 May, 10:42, "Pete Dashwood" <dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> introduced by maintenance work post-implementation.

Don't forget what I consider the root cause of "maintenance-time errors" : 
failure to originally design and code the application with "future 
maintenance" as a criteria.

e.g, "Let's just get it working for now,  we'll make a permanent fix later." 
Except, "later" never happens until "next enhancement"  when the exact same 
thing happens. And the cycle continues.

Sometimes after 'enough' changes you just have to bite the bullet and 
re-write from scratch; but creating the software originally and doing 
ongning maintenance  with an eye toward *future* maintenance can 
dramatically the extend the "pre-write" life of the application.

MCM
```

No offence taken :-) A fine old Saxon epithet I use myself from time to 
time...

Unfortunately (or fortunately, depending on your POV), I have decided not to 
argue this as I really don't have time and it doesn't matter anyway. 
Besides, I doubt that minds are likely to be changed :-)

Obviously, the opinions expressed by me are based on my experience. That 
experience is broad, but certainly not infallible. If you are used to 
working in places where systems are rushed into production and not designed 
and developed properly, then I sympathise. In most of the roles I perform, 
part of responsibility for that deployment rests with me, so I tend to 
ensure it is done properly.


>
>> and, for the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> it can not produce working code?

Yes. You might ask yourself why you'd want to be part of it :-)
>
>> It is when it needs "just this one small
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It is in my experience... There is budget and spotlight on developing 
systems; enhancing and changing them is very much a poor relation, relegated 
to the dead of night very often and with little acknowledgement. All too 
often, a thankless task.

>>
>> Systems that don't require maintenance don't require regression testing
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> regression testing.

Yes, your presumption is accurate :-)
>
>> Encapsulated functionality CANNOT be changed by the introduction of
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> all file types, etc.?

Doesn't happen. Developer A wrote a class to handle file A. If it could be 
generalised he would have done so. If not, then any new requirements to 
access similar files can inherit from class A where appropriate, or will 
require a new class if not. What DOESN'T happen is for someone to tinker 
with Class A  in the hope of changing it's functionality. Class A has not 
changed; the applications which use it don't need to change.
>
>>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> turn the engine over.

Sure. Either way you don't strip the engine down. That was my point.

>
>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>>

I'll be deciding at the end of this month... I'll be the one coming through 
Heathrow in the pointy hat... :-)

>>
>>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Pete.

Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
>"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
>news:1178798582.549183.180370@q75g2000hsh.googlegroups.com...

[snip]

>> More often than not I would say that those who enhance/maintain
>> systems are rarely the saps who wrote the rubbish in the first place
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…

>Besides, I doubt that minds are likely to be changed :-)
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>ensure it is done properly.

Mr Dashwood, the statistic - with varying forms of documentation - has 
been cited before, that n% (n usually being a number greater than fifty 
and less than 80) of Large Systems (a Large System being defined 
as have more than y(10,000) lines of code) never see the light of 
Production.

Given this situation to be valid and given that your experience does not 
reflect it... then it might be concluded that *your* experience is 
anomalous.  The last project you worked on, for instance... that was a 
Large System that never saw the Light of Prod, no?

]snip]

>> What happens when developer A codes OO source to handle file A and
>> then developer B gets tasked with generalising the code to cater for
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>generalised he would have done so.

What, Mr Dashwood?  Just because a programming methodology changes - 
procedural to OO - all incompetent managers disappear?

'Baker, we need an object that's generalised to handle all file types... 
Able was working on this and got hit by a Mack truck so you'll have to 
take over.  He already has something running in Prod that handles file A 
so it'll be easy, all ya gotta do is...'

What a brave, new world that has no such things in it!

>If not, then any new requirements to 
>access similar files can inherit from class A where appropriate, or will 
>require a new class if not. What DOESN'T happen is for someone to tinker 
>with Class A  in the hope of changing it's functionality. Class A has not 
>changed; the applications which use it don't need to change.

What happens often, Mr Dashwood, is that the technology is applied in 
accordance with the understanding of the people controlling it... this is 
why many of the participants here can smile and say 'You worked in a shop 
where the Project Leader didn't allow you to code a SEARCH?  Why, that's 
nothing, on one job I had the Project Leader didn't allow SEARCH, SORT or 
alternate-index VSAM datasets... and she liked to see everything reduced 
to a three-file match/merge program, too, even it the situation didn't 
merit it!'

[snip]

>I'll be deciding at the end of this month... I'll be the one coming through 
>Heathrow in the pointy hat... :-)

May you have a safe journey and a prosperous return, Mr Dashwood... but I 
didn't know Heathrow was sporting such headgear nowadays.

DD

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:f21d9t$o1q$1@reader2.panix.com...
> In article <5ai56cF2oqsosU1@mid.individual.net>,
> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> have I seen that before?

Not a gambit, a simple statement of fact. I wish I had more time to spend 
here; I don't.
>
>>Besides, I doubt that minds are likely to be changed :-)
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Production.

As mentioned by someone else previously.... BOLLOCKS!
>
> Given this situation to be valid and given that your experience does not
> reflect it... then it might be concluded that *your* experience is
> anomalous.  The last project you worked on, for instance... that was a
> Large System that never saw the Light of Prod, no?

Yes and no. It certainly didn't go in where I was working, but  I beleive it 
did in other places. A management decision was taken not to implement, for 
reasons which were mainly political but which, in hindsight, may well have 
been right. I did what I was paid for and kept the options open. I have no 
regrets about it and was well paid for my efforts.

If you are suggesting that systems should always be implemented, even when 
circumstances change, that is a very hard position to defend. Good 
management monitors what is going on and is flexible enough to change a 
policy when it is seen to be no longer relevant.

Some people find change very hard to deal with...

Certainly, it has not been my experience that systems NORMALLY get 
cancelled.


>
> ]snip]
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> procedural to OO - all incompetent managers disappear?

Hobby horse time again?  Excuse me while I snooze...zzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzz
>
[snipped tedious, cynical rehash of ideas that have been done to death here 
many times]
>
>>I'll be deciding at the end of this month... I'll be the one coming 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Oh very droll... having a bad period are we?

Pete.

> A library size can change because new methods/properties/events are added.
