Well, that's it.... and you should be even wondering why Microsoft is
still maintaining (oops, enhancing) their old OO code.

New methods/properties/events are part of "new business process" is
it? That is what I meant by "maintenance" thingy. Apparently, (if you
really are an OO programmer) previous methods need not to be
changed... that's the concept of OO is it? And that point is taken.
When it is deployed and into production.... then it is "assumed" that
it is fully 100% functional. And again that point is taken in OO
programming.

But still you need maintenance.... to enhance it, and even worst...
look at the other method in the OO code that you need encapsulate in
your new method. Well, inheritance is provided anyway in OO right?...
so as an OO programmer, you don't have to reinvent the wheel but
instead use the existing OO methods.

Who doesn't do maintenance nowadays? Even your Ferrari need one.

"Rene_Surop" <infodynamics_ph@yahoo.com> wrote in message 
news:1178803615.058961.34100@e51g2000hsg.googlegroups.com...
> On May 10, 2:42 am, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> changed... that's the concept of OO is it?

That is arguably one of the fundamental reasons for encapsulation, yes. It 
certainly works for me. Components encapsulate functionality. You want to 
change the functionality? You can inherit what you did already, or you can 
write a new Class; what you DON'T do is tinker with code that is already in 
production and functioning correctly. After a while you learn to see your 
Classes (components) as small, tight, pieces of functionality (exactly like 
intrinsic or embedded functions). (I think it is this realization that may 
have led to the new wave of Functional Programming which is creating a lot 
of excitement currently, especially when we look at data access...)

>And that point is taken.
> When it is deployed and into production.... then it is "assumed" that
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> instead use the existing OO methods.

See above.

>
> Who doesn't do maintenance nowadays? Even your Ferrari need one.
>
Not mine. I don't have one... :-)

Pete.
```

On May 11, 11:19 am, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
> That is arguably one of the fundamental reasons for encapsulation, yes. It
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> production and functioning correctly.

_____________________
OO against procedural.

Yes, as an OO programmer I don't.... because you will defeat the
purpose of the object or OO programming in general. I am into OO
programming and I will tell you, OO codes is way way way better off
than procedural when it comes to "maintenance" of codes. It is because
you don't have to touch old/working methods, just enhance (or
maintain) by adding new functionality. And I won't go back to
procedural programming.... and that what we have in common.

______________________
C#, VB.net, Cobol, Java, etc

But taking this topic into context, you mentioned C#, VB.net (OO
programming) against an old Cobol (procedural)..... no way you could
compare this two! That what makes Cobol programmers fight back. It
ALWAYS been the same, and IT people do not get it. Computer language
is a "religious" thing. In this age, well mentioning old dogs and new
puppets.... we don't talk much of computer language anymore....
because once compiled as an OO code, it is GENERIC. You could access
the application in any other application you want, deploy it in any
platform you need.

I'm doing my Cobol code in OO now, instead of C++, C# or VB.net....
why?!?

1. Because of OO programming concept
2. Cobol codes can access ASCII and EBCDIC... even directly to your
mid-range/mainframe platform
3. Codes are very easy to read, it is really matured
4. Cobol code can be "extracted" and can be used as a source code
outside PC platform (going to mainframe)
5. Cobol code or COM objects can be accessed via HTTP protocol (http://
infowaters.infodynamicsconsult.com)
6. Can be used in your HSPDA, EDGE, 3G mobile phones via mobile
browsers
7. and a lot more..... (who knows)

But hey... I still do C++, Java codes. And it works in all cases.
```

On Fri, 11 May 2007 15:19:34 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>
>"Rene_Surop" <infodynamics_ph@yahoo.com> wrote in message 
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>of excitement currently, especially when we look at data access...)

This gets to be interesting.  I have found that many times when
something is used in a new way, interesting things creep out of the
woodwork.  Also, even if you don't change the object, you are changing
the thing that invokes the object.  Something changes, be it a CGI
script, a COBOL/C#/C++/Java module, a HTML web page, etc.  What ever
the entity, something is maintained/modified.  Also each layer of
encapsulation costs.  Even on our much faster systems, at some volume
of activity, this overhead becomes a problem.  Of course we are able
to tolerate a lot more overhead than we used to and to the degree that
overhead is buying advantage (development speed, flexibility, etc.) it
is worth the cost.   
>
>>And that point is taken.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO and change was Re: Cobol News - Microfocus and AcuCOBOL

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-05-15T21:22:23+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5atceeF2qslc4U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<463e4cf9$0$8983$4c368faf@roadrunner.com> <5a8p1fF2mq7k3U1@mid.individual.net> <1178651347.798594.117310@u30g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <5acemfF2oe62dU1@mid.individual.net> <1178693192.699748.101870@e65g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <5adq5bF2oc19aU1@mid.individual.net> <1178767753.307905.221640@w5g2000hsg.googlegroups.com> <5ag7noF2ohe2nU1@mid.individual.net> <1178803615.058961.34100@e51g2000hsg.googlegroups.com> <5ai5m8F2pbgl4U1@mid.individual.net> <fc0i43tm0v1pisvgtnanv2pkful2k3pehk@4ax.com>`

```

"Clark F Morris" <cfmpublic@ns.sympatico.ca> wrote in message 
news:fc0i43tm0v1pisvgtnanv2pkful2k3pehk@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 11 May 2007 15:19:34 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>>
<snip>>
> This gets to be interesting.  I have found that many times when
> something is used in a new way, interesting things creep out of the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> the entity, something is maintained/modified.

Well, what you are talking about is merely "glue". It could be a script or 
it could, itself, be a Class. What it ISN'T, is thousands of lines of code 
embodying Business Rules... The glue is not "maintained" in the way that 
procedural engine room code is maintained, although it can be changed from 
time to time. Furthermore, you may NOT change it when you want to modify its 
behaviour; it may be simpler and easier to replace it. I wouldn't maintain a 
web page originally generated from a template, for example (for anything 
other than the most trivial changes); I'd simply regenerate it.

It isn't about COBOL versus new technology; it is about interpreting new 
technology with an old mind set. As long as you are thinking about "The 
prime importance of maintaining source code..." it really doesn't matter 
whether you use COBOL or OO; either way you are locked into the past and not 
getting the full benefits of the present. There's little point in bewailing 
the uselessness of a scalpel if all you ever do with it is sharpen pencils. 
You should have stuck with turning the little handle on the edge of your 
desk...

What is needed is to think about systems in terms of the FUNCTIONALITY 
required to implement them, not the source code. The functionality is 
determined by an object model, and use cases, and once it is identified it 
can be encapsulated into Classes which can be shared amongst processes. It 
is a different paradigm. (I mentioned oranges and submarines somewhere...)

COBOL aims to solve the maintenance problem by being self-documenting and 
clear (when well written... it doesn't always achieve this).

But modern languages (especially those used for scripting, like C#) have 
trumped this with conciseness and power.

If you have 10 lines of something, even if it isn't immediately English-like 
and intuitive to an untrained person, it is still easier to change than a 
couple of hundred lines of code, which is beautifully documented and uses 
meaningul names etc.

(It is also much quicker (cheaper) to write/generate)


> Also each layer of
> encapsulation costs.

Not necessarily. There is no need for an encapsulated solution to require 
any more tiers than would normally be the case. The fact that presentation, 
logic and data tiers are implemented as components requires no more "layers" 
than any other approach.

>
>Even on our much faster systems, at some volume
> of activity, this overhead becomes a problem.

There are many ways to deal with performance in networked systems. 
Distributed components and web services can be placed where they are most 
needed, on dedicated platforms if necessary.  (I was recently surprised by 
the difference in performance obtained just  by moving a database from a Web 
Server to a Database server, with no change to program code at all.) Again, 
distributed systems are a different paradigm.



>Of course we are able
> to tolerate a lot more overhead than we used to and to the degree that
> overhead is buying advantage (development speed, flexibility, etc.) it
> is worth the cost.

Yes, everything is about balance...

<snip>

Pete.
```

On 8 May 2007 23:46:32 -0700, adestrup@gmail.com wrote:

>Do you really think that C# or VB.NET are the languages of the future?

They are probably less likely even than CoBOL to be "the languages of
the future".

>Twenty years ago I read the same things about C, C++ and VB but that
>didn't happen!
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>the programmer ... a big difference!

But it is a difference that matters less and less.    All around, we
see that maintenance is expensive, it is easier to have tools to
create from scratch.    Throw away your iPod or TV or even trousers
with a hole in the pocket when it stops working and get a new one.

In programming, those tools don't even have to look like languages.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol News - Microfocus and AcuCOBOL

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Clark F Morris <cfmpublic@ns.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2007-05-11T17:47:29-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<poh9439i86lj3vslkod5b6n3o2c1d464h3@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1178289593.102426.302630@u30g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <pNS_h.104316$VU4.101008@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <MtCdnZhWqo6zoKHbnZ2dnUVZ8tSdnZ2d@pipex.net> <1oc%h.265046$c62.228514@fe07.news.easynews.com> <5a62r0F2mo062U1@mid.individual.net> <463e4cf9$0$8983$4c368faf@roadrunner.com> <5a8p1fF2mq7k3U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Tue, 8 May 2007 01:48:29 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>
>"Colin Campbell" <cmcampb@adelphia.net> wrote in message 
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>discussion in 2015...)

While I am as skeptical about the long term future of both COBOL and
the mainframe (primarily IBM, the only one I am familiar with although
Unisys seem to be staying relatively invisible), it is not because of
OO but because of terminal short-sightedness on IBM's part.
>
>I know people who still make lace by hand; that doesn't mean their process 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>afford.

I am fairly certain that the Royal Bank of Canada is doing a large
portion of their bank processing on IBM mainframes using COBOL
including much of the handling of my account.  If the Royal Bank
doesn't have a Net presence, I am doing my online banking by magic.  I
suspect that they are using COBOL and the mainframe where it makes
sense and other platforms and technologies where they make sense.  
>
>The Network has inherited the Earth; COBOL does not cope well with the 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>free...)

COBOL in a sense never did cope that well with online.  CICS has COBOL
modules in a sea of API's where much of the control is done by CICS.
CICS has been modified to talk to the web so the COBOL modules now can
go along for the ride with relatively little modification in some
cases.  
>
>10 - 15 years or so ago, if the COBOL community had embraced OO, the 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>thousands for training.

10 - 15 years ago, OO didn't have that much in terms of function to
offer the mainframe side of the fence and the things that drove people
away from COBOL on the Unix and Windows platforms were already
prevalent.  On the mainframe, the infrastructure to use OO wasn't
there in a usable manner that bought much.  One small example of why
back in the early 1990's OO wouldn't have seemed that important is the
edited move.  In COBOL this is a simple thing native to the language,
especially for the United States.  The need for having an object to do
this was not apparent.  Fast forward to now and the need for
internationalization and the desirability of using an object for
editing becomes far greater.  Unfortunately, we still haven't the
infrastructure on at least the IBM mainframe that would make moving to
OO that feasible.   
>
>Simple Darwinism; fail to adapt, get replaced by a species that did.  COBOL 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>is much easier.

Your description of C# AND ITS SUPPORT FACILITIES makes me want to use
it for anything new and to get similar support facilities for existing
environments.
>
>>I know the organization I retired from is still writing new COBOL programs, 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

"Clark F Morris" <cfmpublic@ns.sympatico.ca> wrote in message 
news:poh9439i86lj3vslkod5b6n3o2c1d464h3@4ax.com...
> On Tue, 8 May 2007 01:48:29 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[56 more quoted lines elided]…
> sense and other platforms and technologies where they make sense.

I think the key here is "makes sense". What "makes sense" today, may not do 
so tomorrow, when there is more awareness of what is available, what is 
available now is much more powerful and improved, and when other competitors 
have upped the ante.

>>
>>The Network has inherited the Earth; COBOL does not cope well with the
…[61 more quoted lines elided]…
>>

Good and fair responses, Clark. I really don't have time to argue this at 
the moment and I don't really take issue with your post anyway.

Whatever the reasons (and yours are every bit as good as mine), COBOL missed 
the boat with OO. I always find that sad because the actual implementation 
of OO facilities into COBOL is a software engineering work of Art.

I predict a move towards the network, even for the major banks, but time 
will tell.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol News - Microfocus and AcuCOBOL

- **From:** Rene_Surop <infodynamics_ph@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2007-05-11T00:26:53-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1178868413.620203.168170@h2g2000hsg.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1178819354@f609.n257.z2.fidonet.ftn>`
- **References:** `<1178289593.102426.302630@u30g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <pNS_h.104316$VU4.101008@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <1178819354@f609.n257.z2.fidonet.ftn>`

```
On May 11, 9:49 am, "Vince Coen" <VBCoenDesp...@btconnect.com> wrote:
>
> Attempting to install this package I notice that it requires MS Visual Studio
> 2005.  I have now installed MS Visual Express but the NetExpress installer
> does not like it and it insists on having visual studio present.
>

Hi Vince,

Try to install the downloaded setup again. But this time when the
installation modules are displayed, DO NOT select the .NET support
module. Here are the steps:

1.  Install NetExpress Personal Edition
2.  Automatically the installation will begin
3.  Once the module window appear;
    3.1.  Deselect the "Net Express Support for .NET" by clicking the
module (set to X)

Then it should work now. If you want .NET support included..... then
"separately download" Microsoft .NET framework and install it first
prior Microfocus NetExpress.

Regards.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol News - Microfocus and AcuCOBOL

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2007-05-17T20:38:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v333i.191859$aG1.27495@pd7urf3no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1179429628.689188.30740@w5g2000hsg.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<1178289593.102426.302630@u30g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <pNS_h.104316$VU4.101008@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <1178819354@f609.n257.z2.fidonet.ftn> <1178868413.620203.168170@h2g2000hsg.googlegroups.com> <1179311010@f609.n257.z2.fidonet.ftn> <1179324007.479795.10960@y80g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <1179429628.689188.30740@w5g2000hsg.googlegroups.com>`

```
Alistair wrote:
> On 16 May, 15:00, Alistair <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> 
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

So now you've got it downloaded what are you going to do with it, (a) 
Procedural, (b) OO COBOL without .Net or (c) OO COBOL and .Net ?

Did you ever get a second-hand copy of Arranga/Coyle from amazon.com for 
about $20 ?

As stated previously, they , (Arranga/Coyle), have a small application 
trying to address as many OO features as possible. (It is an in-house 
library application for corporate staff to borrow books and videos, and 
you pay fines if returns are late back). To simplify comprehension they 
did the following :-

- No GUIs or Screen Section - substituted straight ANSI displays

- File records are held in W/S Data Tables

Unfortunately a lot of typing involved, (I never had luck getting either 
author to produce the diskette containing their source). Type it up and 
it should work fine in V 5.0 without .Net. With .Net the *first* change 
is that you rename FACTORY to read STATIC.

If you go .Net route, can't help you much without I also download the 
Personal Edition - but of course I would then need to buy Visual Studio, 
plus get a handle on the 'system.calls' associated with C#, and learn C#.

Now if you want to type the source as is, (brave man :-) ) - leave out 
the comment statements, so that it runs solely in an OO COBOL 
environment, pass me the stuff as a zip file and I'll spruce it up, 
(dropping a lot of the 'red tape', which makes it more readable), plus I 
could add COBOL files/SQL Tables or both (?) to replace the W/S Data 
Tables. The ANSI displays could be turned into Screen Section or a 
simple GUI set.

If you get it working in OO COBOL without .Net then it shouldn't be a 
major change to dabble and switch it to .Net later, using .BASIC say to 
replace Screen Section or GUI classes.

You've got bugger all else to do - so start typing :-)

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol News - Microfocus and AcuCOBOL

- **From:** Rene_Surop <infodynamics_ph@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2007-05-07T10:19:49-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1178558389.392931.16940@n59g2000hsh.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<5a8pbuF2ndiqhU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1178289593.102426.302630@u30g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <gfudnS4TPYwbWaPbnZ2dnUVZ_u3inZ2d@comcast.com> <5a8pbuF2ndiqhU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
>
> Assuming the above IS possible, if I write a web service in NetExpress COBOL
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

Good question Pete.

However, Microfocus is always ahead when talking about licensing fees.
Web Services or wrapped Microfocus Cobol COM objects tied to your web
server need an Application Server runtime license.... well, what I
knew is that is it capable of handling 10users (at the start/first
buy). So basically if your Web Server is expecting to hit as much
users it can process, you have to buy "additional" licensing fees for
additional users. I do not know how are they controlling the whole
thing... but you have to pay a hefty runtime fees for your hefty user
hits. There is always a quick response for Microfocus on this issue.

The question here is, if at first you're licensed to 10users.... how
will it treat your 11th, 12th, 13th or so users at the back-end? Would
there be a queue?


Rene Surop
```

### Posting on rail news group Re: NYCTA Computer usage was Re: Fate of RCA computer division was Re: P.A. systems in large railroad stations?

- **From:** Clark F Morris <cfmpublic@ns.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2007-05-08T19:32:09-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hcu143dqa0oeu8m925b6g9k35gd9di4c59@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1178204619.754387.98340@e65g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <463A1753.8040702@earthlink.net> <1178293333.855579.281120@l77g2000hsb.googlegroups.com> <463B75AC.60209@earthlink.net> <1178304148.841271.99200@p77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <pjss33958in5a99nda7acjbpvg5njpp0h5@4ax.com> <463e941a$0$4722$4c368faf@roadrunner.com> <1178558626.517556.37950@w5g2000hsg.googlegroups.com> <nasadowsk-3044FF.18233507052007@news.verizon.net> <1178636980.665443.61580@o5g2000hsb.googlegroups.com>`

```
The following on misc.transport.rail.americas may be the main reason
traditional IT departments have been losing out.  Note that Walmart
has a bunch of mainframes and probably still does COBOL when they
can't get a package to do what they want but the IT department works
with the other departments and then goes back to make sure that what
developed actually works for those who have to work with the products.
That is more important than whether they go OO or not.

On 8 May 2007 08:09:40 -0700, hancock4@bbs.cpcn.com wrote:

>On May 7, 6:23 pm, Philip Nasadowski <nasado...@usermale.com> wrote:
>
…[88 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Posting on rail news group Re: NYCTA Computer usage was Re: Fate of RCA computer division was Re: P.A. systems in large railroad stations?

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2007-05-09T13:16:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f1shk5$ib7$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<1178204619.754387.98340@e65g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <nasadowsk-3044FF.18233507052007@news.verizon.net> <1178636980.665443.61580@o5g2000hsb.googlegroups.com> <hcu143dqa0oeu8m925b6g9k35gd9di4c59@4ax.com>`

```
In article <hcu143dqa0oeu8m925b6g9k35gd9di4c59@4ax.com>,
Clark F Morris  <cfmpublic@ns.sympatico.ca> wrote:
>The following on misc.transport.rail.americas may be the main reason
>traditional IT departments have been losing out.  Note that Walmart
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>developed actually works for those who have to work with the products.

Hmmmmm... I seem to recall a series of one-panel cartoons about such 
processes, it had something to do with a back-yard swing.

[attributions in the following text may be botched; my apologies to anyone 
to whom this may give offense]

>That is more important than whether they go OO or not.
>
>On 8 May 2007 08:09:40 -0700, hancock4@bbs.cpcn.com wrote:
>
>>On May 7, 6:23 pm, Philip Nasadowski <nasado...@usermale.com> wrote:

[snip]

>>> ... A big problem with DP in an industrial setting seems to be lots of data,
>>> but no useful information from it. ...
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>>because they drown in information they don't need nor want.

I would ask... which is worse, 'flooding people with paper or screens' or 
getting fired for insubordination?  Manager (x) tells you 'I want this'; 
after a suitable number of 'what do you need it for?' responses Manager 
(x) may tell *your* manager 'All I wanna do is get my report and your 
programmer is being uncooperative' and your manager says 'Give him what 
he's asking for and stop being a nuisance'.

>>
>>We had an accountant who prepared financial reports on Excel to a lay
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>>whatsoever to the board.

I would say it is up to The Board to determine what they consider worth 
spending their time on or not; the accountant did what he saw as his job 
and the Board Members did what they saw to be theirs.

[snip]

>>Back in the 1970s when we learned systems development it was
>>importatant to define all system outputs.  WHO would get the reports/
>>screens?  WHAT would the person do with it?  WHEN did he need it?  WHY
>>did he need it?  This got the right information to the right people at
>>the right time.

This takes Work and this takes Time.  User (n) might complain that 
'something I do in seconds on a spreadsheet takes months to implement on 
the mainframe'... spreadsheets, on the other hand, don't throw an error 
when you enter an invalid Customer Number.

>>
>>Good systems work is an interitive process.  After a new system is up
>>and running people will discover new questions and new ways of doing
>>things.  The system must evolve to reflect that and not be locked in
>>stone.

It must not be so free as to allow for changes which cannot be shown to 
have a positive Return on Investment, either, or else money disappears.

(ROI analysis?  How quaint... who does *that* any more?)

(one must also be cautious about 'feature bloat'... I've sat through a 
series of Requirements Meetings where folks would say 'But the new system 
doesn't do (q)... and the new system doesn't do (r)... and couldn't we get 
the new system to do (m), too?'... and I've seen these same folks then say 
during testing 'Wow, there's an awful lot of crap in here, why can't we 
just Do Our Jobs?')

[snip]

>>I found a book written in 1940 that explains the system well.  If a
>>govt administrator comes up with a new idea that works--and many do--
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>>summer or perhaps too well decorated for a 'govt office'.

You have *got* to be kidding.  True, there are not that many newspaper 
reporters covering how the CEO of GiantoCorp decorates her office... but 
if someone believes that in the private sector one can blithely make 
mistakes that Cost Real Money without a risk of 'Remeber the Dithers 
fiasco?' (or a variant thereof) being mentioned has experiences much 
different than mine.

[snip]

>>BTW, contracting out work or a whole operation to the private sector
>>does not improve things, indeed, it often makes things worse.

Just as a society can be seen as being reflected in its institutions 
(corporate, government, charitable, etc.) so can a corporation can be seen 
as being reflected in its contractors.  After all... you don't want to 
hire someone *too* different, do you?  They have to... Fit In.

('Fit In... to what?'  'Fit In... to the Way Things Are Done Around Here, 
of course'  'But... aren't we hiring contractors because the Way Things 
Are Done Around Here is failing to meet the need?'  'Wow... you have a 
Really Bad Attitude, you know... this project needs Team Players.')

DD
```

>> Back in the 1970s when we learned systems development it was
>> importatant to define all system outputs.  WHO would get the reports/
>> screens?  WHAT would the person do with it?  WHEN did he need it?  WHY
>> did he need it?  This got the right information to the right people at
>> the right time.

That stuff should still be taught.  "Well, they *might* need it" is no 
excuse for a cluttered report.  Write it modularly, and if the "might" 
becomes a "do", flip a switch and the new data appears.

I know that can be done because I have been on projects where it was 
done.  :)
```

---

### OT: fished from a long thread

- **From:** Michael Russell <Michael.Russell@msn.com>
- **Date:** 2007-05-10T22:09:19+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TNmdncrIdfJhFN7bRVnyuwA@pipex.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
 >
 > Democracy has to work, because all the alternatives are not 
even options, for free people.
 >
 > Pete.

(Perhaps people would like to pick up this topic - after all, 
these terms seem practically unchallengeable, but who knows 
what they mean?)

It would be interesting to hear what 'democracy' and 'free' mean.

As far as I can tell, democracy means being able to vote for 
any of the candidates put before you .... by the oligarchy.

As for free - what comes first, civil freedom or economic freedom?
How do we see freedom 'in action', please?

It's a nice notion and kind of has an axiomatic ring - 
democracy has to work for free people - but I think it's a 
myth, so I don't subscribe.

If you subscribe to the idea, do please elucidate these 
fundamentals.

Michael
```

#### ↳ Re: fished from a long thread

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-05-11T15:58:37+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5ai7veF2oj8ngU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<TNmdncrIdfJhFN7bRVnyuwA@pipex.net>`

```

"Michael Russell" <Michael.Russell@msn.com> wrote in message 
news:TNmdncrIdfJhFN7bRVnyuwA@pipex.net...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> It would be interesting to hear what 'democracy' and 'free' mean.

As the OP on this, and with due deference to Wittgenstein, I was using 
'Democracy' in the sense of a system of government whereby issues are 
explored and considered, then voted on by the populace. The majority decide 
which option will be taken, with due regard so far as possible, to the 
feelings of the minority, and the minority accept the decision, knowing they 
will be free to persuade and force another vote after an agreed period of 
time.

The minority do NOT decide that if the vote doesn't go their way they will 
attempt to enforce their will on the majority by means of terror, civil 
unrest/disobediance, or any other form of disruption. The sole recourse of a 
minority in a Democracy is to change minds by peaceful means, and get 
another vote.

By 'free people' I am referring to people who have freedoms 'from' and 
freedoms 'to'.

Within the constraint of not injuring others, and obeying the agreements the 
society has agreed to (the Law) such people are unhindered in thought, word, 
and action. Their movement is unrestrained, they may say and think whatever 
they want to, and practise whatever belief systems they do or do not hold. 
They are 'free' to pursue their lives and raise their children in peace and 
security. Usually, such people have respect for their fellows and good will 
towards them (although these attributes are not a requirement for freedom).

Democracy, while not perfect, is the system which best accommodates such 
people. Free people need to be responsible for their own lives and futures; 
no other system of Government allows personal responsibility and choice.


>
> As far as I can tell, democracy means being able to vote for any of the 
> candidates put before you .... by the oligarchy.

No, see above. There is a real danger that this could happen, and it is one 
of the things democracies need to be on guard against. Freedom brings 
responsibility. Stop being responsible, you may well lose your freedom.
>
> As for free - what comes first, civil freedom or economic freedom?
> How do we see freedom 'in action', please?
>

The fact that you can post these thoughts, and I can read them and respond 
to them, without ANYONE censoring or moderating them, is just one aspect of 
'freedom in action'. To answer your question, civil freedom is first and 
must always be so. Economic freedom is a choice you make. In a free society, 
you have the opportunity to ensure your economic freedom, but only if you 
have civil freedom to do so.

> It's a nice notion and kind of has an axiomatic ring - democracy has to 
> work for free people - but I think it's a myth, so I don't subscribe.

I respect your right to feel that way :-) (Besides, I'm not an 
Evangelist...my OP was a comment in passing...) Hopefully, these 
interchanges may stimulate both of us to re-evaluate what we think.
>
> If you subscribe to the idea, do please elucidate these fundamentals.
>

I certainly do subscribe to what I write, otherwise I wouldn't post it. 
Unfortunately, irrespective of how passionate I may feel, my time is limited 
so there is only so much I can write here. I'd love to be able to sit and 
discuss ideas all day on CLC :-) I can't. If I wish to retain my economic 
freedom (and I do... it took me many years to attain it... :-)), I need to 
channel some of my time into places where they pay me for it :-).

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: fished from a long thread

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2007-05-11T08:54:42-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h8094358bctan7uudumtjqhh3785sfi06s@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<TNmdncrIdfJhFN7bRVnyuwA@pipex.net> <5ai7veF2oj8ngU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Fri, 11 May 2007 15:58:37 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>As the OP on this, and with due deference to Wittgenstein, I was using 
>'Democracy' in the sense of a system of government whereby issues are 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>time.

So this is pretty much limited to very small organizations where
issues are open and easy to see, facts obtainable, and agreement (for
the period of time) not established by previous generations.

>The minority do NOT decide that if the vote doesn't go their way they will 
>attempt to enforce their will on the majority by means of terror, civil 
>unrest/disobediance, or any other form of disruption. The sole recourse of a 
>minority in a Democracy is to change minds by peaceful means, and get 
>another vote.

So a minority can lose their freedoms if a majority wills it?    Civil
disobedience has its place.

>By 'free people' I am referring to people who have freedoms 'from' and 
>freedoms 'to'.

No one has ever had absolute freedom "from" and "to", even within the
constraint listed below.

>Within the constraint of not injuring others, and obeying the agreements the 
>society has agreed to (the Law) such people are unhindered in thought, word, 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>towards them (although these attributes are not a requirement for freedom).

Peace and security are not absolute terms.    Especially when the
argument is how much of our rights we need to give up for security.

>Democracy, while not perfect, is the system which best accommodates such 
>people. Free people need to be responsible for their own lives and futures; 
>no other system of Government allows personal responsibility and choice.

But some have been more effective at offering peace and security. And
certainly an effective Constitution is useful at limiting a Democracy,
allowing better protection of our rights.

>> As far as I can tell, democracy means being able to vote for any of the 
>> candidates put before you .... by the oligarchy.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>responsibility. Stop being responsible, you may well lose your freedom.

Yep.   But what can I do to stop the political process from being an
election of the powerful (money is a subset of power)?

>> As for free - what comes first, civil freedom or economic freedom?
>> How do we see freedom 'in action', please?
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>have civil freedom to do so.

To some extent.   There are constraints on both freedoms.   The forms
of these constraints vary from place to place and from time to time.


I'm sort of Libertarian.   Sure, if Libertarians would be elected,
they would pass some laws I don't like before they got corrupted. But
I'd like many aspects of our (I'm a US citizen) current system, to
move in that direction.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: fished from a long thread

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()

- **References:** `<TNmdncrIdfJhFN7bRVnyuwA@pipex.net> <5ai7veF2oj8ngU1@mid.individual.net> <h8094358bctan7uudumtjqhh3785sfi06s@4ax.com> <13496skgbng4ed3@corp.supernews.com>`

```
In article <13496skgbng4ed3@corp.supernews.com>,
Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
>
>"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>-- Benjamin Franklin

Cite, please?  Nothing personal intended, Mr Smith, just a matter of 
Academic Integrity, as what follows will, I hope, demonstrate.

A quick web-search found this formulation only on 
<https://www.cpa2biz.com/News/Telberg/The+IRS+Defending+Privacy+or+Fighting+Terror.htm> 
and while the AICPA (American Institute of Certified Public Accountants) 
may be a precise group I question the quality of their scholarship... in 
this case.

According to http://www.bartleby.com/73/1056.html the quote is 'Those who 
would give up essential Liberty, to purchase a little temporary Safety, 
deserve neither Liberty nor Safety.' .

On the other hand... http://www.bartleby.com/100/245.html (quoting 
Bartlett's 10th ed, 1919) has the Ellis Island version of 'They that can 
give up essential liberty to obtain a little temporary safety deserve 
neither liberty nor safety.'

(the footnote to the second Bartleby cite makes reference to the first 
formulation's source (Pennsylvania Assembly: Reply to the Governor, 
November 11, 1755)... so it might be that Mr Franklin formulated the same 
sentiments with different particulars; Mr Smith's version is the first 
I've seen using weights... or maybe combining weights and currencies, 
given the differing interpretations of 'pound' common during those days)

('In for a penny, in for twelve Troy ounces.')

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: fished from a long thread

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2007-05-11T14:01:24-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1349c26mvqo1g63@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<TNmdncrIdfJhFN7bRVnyuwA@pipex.net> <5ai7veF2oj8ngU1@mid.individual.net> <h8094358bctan7uudumtjqhh3785sfi06s@4ax.com> <13496skgbng4ed3@corp.supernews.com> <f2280l$1eg$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:f2280l$1eg$1@reader2.panix.com...
> In article <13496skgbng4ed3@corp.supernews.com>,
> Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Cite, please?

<
http://groups.google.com/group/comp.lang.cobol/tree/browse_frm/thread/5f23c7
8eb2bbd731/25f03ffe81dd47e3?rnum=61&hl=en&q=freedom+security+franklin&_done=
%2Fgroup%2Fcomp.lang.cobol%2Fbrowse_frm%2Fthread%2F5f23c78eb2bbd731%2Fa28bc0
b8c19e997d%3Flnk%3Dgst%26q%3Dfreedom%2Bsecurity%2Bfranklin%26rnum%3D1%26hl%3
Den%26#doc_4a252dd325c98707 >
but, at this point in the thread you were requesting
that "SkippyPB" supply a source other than
"some quotes I had stashed away".

< http://en.wikiquote.org/wiki/Benjamin_Franklin >
-----
Those who would give up Essential Liberty to purchase
a little Temporary Safety, deserve neither Liberty nor Safety.

This statement was used as a motto on the title page of An
Historical Review of the Constitution and Government of
Pennsylvania. (1759) which was attributed to Franklin in the
edition of 1812, but in a letter of September 27, 1760 to
David Hume, he states that he published this book and denies
that he wrote it, other than a few remarks that were credited
to the Pennsylvania Assembly, in which he served. The phrase
itself was first used in a letter from that Assembly dated
November 11, 1755 to the Governor of Pennsylvania. An
article on the origins of this statement here includes a scan that
indicates the original typography of the 1759 document, which
uses an archaic form of "s": "Tho?e who would give up Essential
Liberty to purcha?e a little Temporary Safety, deserve neither
Liberty nor Safety." Researchers now believe that a fellow
diplomat by the name of Richard Jackson is the primary author
of the book. With the information thus far available the issue of
authorship of the statement is not yet definitely resolved, but the
evidence indicates it was very likely Franklin, who in the Poor
Richard's Almanack of 1738 is known to have written a similar
proverb: "Sell not virtue to purchase wealth, nor Liberty to
purchase power."

Many paraphrased variants derived from this saying have
arisen and have usually been incorrectly attributed to Franklin:

"They that can give up essential liberty to purchase a little
temporary safety, deserve neither liberty nor safety."
"They that can give up essential liberty to obtain a little
temporary safety deserve neither liberty nor safety."
"Those Who Sacrifice Liberty For Security Deserve Neither"
"He who would trade liberty for some temporary security,
deserves neither liberty nor security"
"He who sacrifices freedom for security deserves neither"
"People willing to trade their freedom for temporary security
deserve neither and will lose both."
"If we restrict liberty to attain security we will lose them both."
"Any society that would give up a little liberty to gain a little
security will deserve neither and lose both."
"He who gives up freedom for safety deserves neither"
-----

Thus, it would seem that the attribution should note
that it is a variation on words by Richard Jackson.
```

--begin quoted text:

Franklin may well have composed this particular quote. The actual version, 
as opposed to the one presented at 44,000 websites, does have the ring of 
his style. A little more more research as to who was present at the 
Assembly November 11, 1755, the minutes of the meeting, and a copy of the 
original letter, should resolve that detail.

--end quoted text

Then again... what is Life without a bit of Mystery?

DD

>> ('In for a penny, in for twelve Troy ounces.')
>
>There are 13 Troy Ounces to the Pound (Gold measures).

Odd... granted that I've not kept up on such things, there may have been a 
bit of inflation since I last looked and my memory is, admittedly, 
porous... but I recall an avoirdupois ounce being 438 grains and sixteen 
to a pound of 7,000 grains... although right there is trouble, if an ounce 
is 438 then a pound would be 7,008 and if a pound is 7,000 then an ounce 
would have to be 437.5... and the troy system had 'heavy ounces, light 
pounds' of 480-grain ounces and 5760-grain pounds (12oz/lb).

pound Troy.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: fished from a long thread

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-05-15T10:29:42+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5as66lF2plln3U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<TNmdncrIdfJhFN7bRVnyuwA@pipex.net> <5ai7veF2oj8ngU1@mid.individual.net> <h8094358bctan7uudumtjqhh3785sfi06s@4ax.com> <13496skgbng4ed3@corp.supernews.com> <f2280l$1eg$1@reader2.panix.com> <1179056836.870915.193140@e65g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <f6tg431ovbiu9dcikemnm3uvaghdhno43j@4ax.com>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:f6tg431ovbiu9dcikemnm3uvaghdhno43j@4ax.com...
> On 13 May 2007 04:47:16 -0700, Alistair
> <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> So which is heavier, a ton of feathers, or a ton of gold?

Oops, sorry about blank reply post. The cursor was on send and I knocked the 
notebook... :-)

Your comment reminded me about something that happened many years ago when I 
was a teenager attending an academic institution in the almost futile hope 
of learning to become a teacher.

We had a Fijian guy in our class who, in order to spare him any 
embarrassment, I shall refer to as "Buck".

Buck, like most of us, had a bright enquiring mind but it was tempered with 
extreme distrust of white people and the need to question intently anything 
proposed by such a person.

He wasn't buying "White Man's magic...".

One day in the Physics lab the discussion turned to the famous experiment 
where a feather and a penny, dropped in a vacuum, fall at the same rate. 
Most of us had either encountered it before or could see that it would work, 
but Buck wasn't buying it.

After about 5 minutes of piercing, pointless questions regarding the nature 
of feathers, pennies, vacums, and the fact that what was being proposed was 
demonstrably untrue because Buck had encountered both pennies and feathers 
and knew which way the wind blew, the lecturer, Mr. Z (who seldom suffered 
fools gladly) was losing patience.

"OK, Buck, you can do the experiment yourself during the lunch break. Here, 
I'll even give you a feather (rummaging in desk draw) and a penny (rummaging 
in copius pants pockets)".

Buck accepted both then said: "That's all very well, but where will I get a 
vacuum?"

Voice from the back..."Use your head, man, use your head..."

Pete.
```

On 11 May, 15:54, Howard Brazee <how...@brazee.net> wrote:
> On Fri, 11 May 2007 15:58:37 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"

> >By 'free people' I am referring to people who have freedoms 'from' and
> >freedoms 'to'.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No-one has freedom. We are all programmed from birth, by society and
by self, to behave in certain ways and we choose to live within our
programming. To be free would require substantial mental re-
programming such as provided by Dianetics:

>From Wikipedia: "Dianetics is a set of ideas and practices regarding
the relationship between mind and body that were developed by science
fiction author L. Ron Hubbard. Hubbard put forth the view that most
mental and physical problems are caused by traumatic memories (which
he called "engrams") that are stored in the unconscious mind, which he
terms the "reactive mind." The goal of Dianetics is to become rid (or
"cleared") of this portion of one's mind. Once at this state of
"Clear," according to Hubbard, an individual becomes able to function
at his or her full potential."

I hate to advocate a quackery such as Scientology but LRH was on to
something there.
```

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2007-05-15T12:03:36+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<f2c7io$9rj$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<TNmdncrIdfJhFN7bRVnyuwA@pipex.net> <f270gu$7h3$1@reader2.panix.com> <egH1i.487248$Pi4.334338@newsfe14.lga> <1179225788.220438.158490@p77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com>`

In article <1179225788.220438.158490@p77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com>,
Alistair  <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>On 13 May, 17:44, "tlmfru" <l...@mts.net> wrote:
>> A lie obviously has to refer back to some real thing or event, or else it's
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>the truth?

Answering a question with a question, Mr Maclean, is no answer at all... 
what I was taught lo, those many decades ago, was that intent to deceive 
was integral to the definition of a lie but at least one definition of 
'lie' from a readily-accepted source appears to say 'it is... and it 
ain't'.  From http://www.m-w.com, lie[4,noun]:

--begin quoted text:

1 a : an assertion of something known or believed by the speaker to be 
untrue with intent to deceive b : an untrue or inaccurate statement that 
may or may not be believed true by the speaker

--end quoted text

DD
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: fished from a long thread

- **From:** Michael Russell <Michael.Russell@msn.com>
- **Date:** 2007-05-11T18:40:04+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OoKdnTzdifruN9nbnZ2dnUVZ8vSdnZ2d@pipex.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<5ai7veF2oj8ngU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<TNmdncrIdfJhFN7bRVnyuwA@pipex.net> <5ai7veF2oj8ngU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> 
  Economic freedom is a choice you make. In a free society,
> you have the opportunity to ensure your economic freedom, but only if you 
> have civil freedom to do so.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Please do not take offense if I seem to smile wryly.

I think people do not really see much of the set-up re economics.

Put briefly, we have no choice about economic freedom; it's 
just not available, that's it.

We live in a fairly well tuned economic arrangement that 
ensures the significant majority (I'm talking 97.99%) will be 
rewarded as _employees_ with no choice in the matter.

(Do people have any idea what the alternative really could be? 
Try starting with Ricardo)

Furthermore, what people earn as an employee is governed by the 
leverage the employer gets from their work - so, no dazzling 
examples of twenty-two or so $M10 wage-earners, please.

There is a vast area that few people consider. It's so vast, 
you wonder just why those dear, caring, philanthropic, 
admirable Founding Fathers just happened to *completely 
overlook it* - apparently.

Yet they were wise, were they not? Very cautious and 
pains-taking to get the tremendously important Constitution 
constructed just so.

When a group of people meet together and make arrangements for 
the benefit of themselves and all the other people not present 
(mandated or as representatives, whatever; integrity is the 
key!) then, if they are not to take advantage of the 
opportunity afforded them, the majority must be agreeing 
saints! There were no saints in attendance in 1787.

It is vast - the area I referred to above. Its good parts are 
both huge and owned & no-one wants the bad parts (because they 
really are bad).

Give in? It's not that hard - just a four letter word.

'Land' - does it get mentioned?

  ... and where & what is the free land available to the 
citizen who doesn't wish to be another employee?

Own the land & you own the economy. It's your constitutional right.

How are you going to get that addressed?
Should be easy in a democracy, right? Well, it was almost a 
democracy in 1787, but they cheered the paper put before them, 
didn't they? No-one quite explained how the rich would inherit 
the earth ... eventually, as they did.

You have Democrats & Republicans.
They argue over the fringes - there's an unspoken agreement 
not, overall, to rock this boat.

If anyone thinks they have civil freedom & economic freedom, 
start looking around.

Michael
```

#### ↳ Re: fished from a long thread

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybubNOSPAM@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2007-05-11T09:09:34-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1348u8o56tjvl96@news.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<TNmdncrIdfJhFN7bRVnyuwA@pipex.net>`

```
Michael Russell wrote:

>> options, for free people.

You can have democracy or freedom - not both.

Roger While wrote:
> Can anybody get this to comnpile ?
> It seems to be missing some periods (or should that be dots)
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Compiled without errors in Net Express 3.1. So you know what I did - I 
used the 'blue' download feature at the bottom which gave me a page with 
the program source; then I said save as 'Hanoi.cbl'. I created Project 
Hanoi, added Hanoi.cbl to it and then compiled.

Just in case you are interested in other 'Towers of Hanoi'. You'll have 
to do some digging in archives but back about 10 years ago there was 
some lively discussion on Towers of Hanoi in this group.

One contributor had a Scandinavian name, (but US citizen, I believe). 
Any old hands remember his name ? (Erickson ???? ). Might give Roger 
something he can hook onto in a search.

Jimmy
```

<a id="t-9331a3f3db"></a>
## Thread: OT: War, Peace, Freedom, Democracy, the US - and various other threads — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT: War, Peace, Freedom, Democracy, the US - and various other threads

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2007-05-14T09:44:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IbW1i.12908$MW1.5401@fe03.news.easynews.com>`

```
I have NOT been participating in the various threads on the various OT topics. 
As an American pacifist (as stated previously in this forum),  I didn't think 
that restating my views would add greatly to (other than to the length of)  any 
of the threads.

As someone who can no longer "read for pleasure" - I do listen to a lot of books 
on tape.  By pure chance, before any of these threads started, I "ordered" (from 
my local library) a copy of Gore Vidal's "Golden Age".  Listen to it as I read 
many of the OT CLC threads - from those within and not within the US (and within 
and not within the military - currently or in the past), I found that *NOVEL* 
quite interesting and relevant.  I think it speaks rather directly to many of 
the posts and wonder if others have read it and agree.

As much as I am against wars (current and past, "justified" by truth or 
non-truths, whatever) I do find it particularly chilling that in the last 
chapter one of the "comments" on loss of freedom had to do with security checks 
before getting on planes. (The book has a copyright date in 2000 and was clearly 
reflecting at lest SOME people's view that doing security checks for internal US 
flights was an unwarranted intrusion.  Ineffective they may have been in the 
early 2000's but without justification is less hard to state given 9/11).
```

---

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5aqr89F2qf93pU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Can someone confirm for me that Conditional compilation is not available in 
Enterprise COBOL for z/OS?

I suspect its not since I can't find it in the manual but cannot find a 
definitive answer anywhere.

TIA
```

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>

- **References:** `<5aqr89F2qf93pU1@mid.individual.net>`

discussion of having a SHARE requirement to add it, but that the user community 
was quite "luke warm" on the topic.
```

>>> On 5/14/2007 at 12:32 PM, in message
<6X12i.379627$9i2.98815@fe06.news.easynews.com>, William M.
Klein<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
> Yes, I can confirm that it is NOT in Enterprise COBOL.  I think there was

> 
> discussion of having a SHARE requirement to add it, but that the user 
> community 
> was quite "luke warm" on the topic.

I'm assuming we're talking about being able to put things like ">>IF
SOMETHING" and ">>ENDIF" around pieces of code and then setting "SOMETHING"
to TRUE or FALSE depending on whether or not we want to compile that section
of code.

Were people against it or just not sure of it's usefulness?  I've wished for
it for years!

Frank
```

MCM

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Conditional compliation

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2007-05-15T08:58:02-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4649761A.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`
- **References:** `<5aqr89F2qf93pU1@mid.individual.net> <6X12i.379627$9i2.98815@fe06.news.easynews.com> <4648890E.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <_562i.3542$mR2.1734@newssvr22.news.prodigy.net>`

```
>>> On 5/14/2007 at 5:01 PM, in message
<_562i.3542$mR2.1734@newssvr22.news.prodigy.net>, Michael
Mattias<mmattias@talsystems.com> wrote:
> "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote in message 
> news:4648890E.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com...
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>> Were people against it or just not sure of it's usefulness?  I've wished

> 
>> for
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 123456D  DISPLAY "Debugging Line"

Yeah, but then you have to put the indicator on each line, instead of just
surrounding a conditional block of code.  Plus its either on or off.  With
">>IF" you can have different conditionals (">>IF DEBUG1", ">>IF DEBUG2"
etc.)

Frank
```

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f2dmia$jkb$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<5aqr89F2qf93pU1@mid.individual.net> <4649761A.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <KYj2i.8950$rO7.5035@newssvr25.news.prodigy.net> <4649EF9C.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`

```
In article <4649EF9C.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>,
Frank Swarbrick <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote:
>>>> On 5/15/2007 at 9:03 AM, in message
><KYj2i.8950$rO7.5035@newssvr25.news.prodigy.net>, Michael
>Mattias<mmattias@talsystems.com> wrote:

[snippage about DEBUGGING MODE and 'D' in column 8]

>> But it beats the snot out of all the other offerings so far, doesn't it?
>
>Yes.  Though to be honest, I've never used it.  I just comment out the
>code.

Ahhhhhh, Mr Swarbrick, there's another advantage to WITH DEBUGGING MODE... 
once, long ago, I took a long weekend... and came back to work to a *very* 
angry Project Leader.  He'd been pressured by the users to slam a fix into 
Prod... and he took the program out of my library, without my knowledge or 
consent, and implemented it.

It did Bad Things, as a work-in-progress might... and the Project Leader 
asked me why I was leaving such dangerous code hanging around because 
someone might just slam it into Prod, as he had, and Bad Things would 
result... in other words, he was trying to make it out to be my fault that 
the code was not labelled as Not Yet Ready so even though I hadn't 
informed him it was Not Yet Ready it wasn't... Obvious.

I brought the source up in editor and said 'See?  Right there, right at 
the top... WITH DEBUGGING MODE, for everyone to see.  You want to put 
debugging code into Prod, you deal with the headaches.'

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Conditional compliation

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2007-05-16T09:32:06-06:00

I'm the only person around here who uses WITH DEBUGGING MODE.

One difficulty with it is that my pre-compiler is run before the
compiler, and it's not smart enough to see whether debugging mode is
on.   So I can't use a D before IDMS statements.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Conditional compliation

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Robert Jones <rjones0@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2007-05-16T12:18:05-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1179343085.642427.216410@y80g2000hsf.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<4649EF9C.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`
- **References:** `<5aqr89F2qf93pU1@mid.individual.net> <6X12i.379627$9i2.98815@fe06.news.easynews.com> <4648890E.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <_562i.3542$mR2.1734@newssvr22.news.prodigy.net> <4649761A.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <KYj2i.8950$rO7.5035@newssvr25.news.prodigy.net> <4649EF9C.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`

```
On May 16, 12:36 am, "Frank Swarbrick"
<Frank.Swarbr...@efirstbank.com> wrote:
> >>> On 5/15/2007 at 9:03 AM, in message
>
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> Frank

The 2002 standard states that debugging lines and DEBUGGING MODE are
now obsolete and are due to be removed from the next (2010?) standard.

I have used it quite a lot myself because I found it very useful.
Although one can use interactive debuggers, which have become much
more reliable over the years, it can be hard to thoroughly check all
relevant work areas during the course of execution when stepping
through interactively, while having a listing (it doesn't have to be
printed) of snapshots taken at judicious points of execution can helo
make such checking more thorough.  My personal preference is to use
both facilities when and as appropriate.  The "D" before each
debugging line does make it clear what is a debugging line, which
conditional compilation doesn't in the same way.  If I ever wrote
programs again, I would probably also use conditional compilation as
well, when it becomes available.  One problem to be wary of with
either debugging lines or conditonal compilation, is ensuring that
final testing of the program with and without the debugging lines is
comprehensive, it can be an easy mistake to have something set in a
debugging line that is required when the debugging facility is turned
off.  Self discipline can mitigate this risk.

Robert
```

It was a ...er....um...."joke"... Frank. :-)

If we're being serious, I agree that something allowing you to turn large 
sections of code on or off, or some state in between, would probably be 
useful for debugging. The danger is that it gets transferred to production 
code and then it is just plain confusing. (The old, "fix this at 2:00 am, 
having never seen it before" syndrome...)

I have worked with compilers that did implement conditional compilation (I 
think NCR did back in the old days and I have a feeling that CDC did too but 
it was a long tine ago and I might be getting confused with macro assemblers 
which certainly did), and never needed to use it.

Given the "multi-line" nature of COBOL I guess there is a case for it.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Conditional compliation

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2007-05-15T19:26:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NPn2i.54282$LM1.33387@fe01.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<5aqr89F2qf93pU1@mid.individual.net> <6X12i.379627$9i2.98815@fe06.news.easynews.com> <4648890E.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`

```
My (vague) memory was that IBM customers that I talked about thought it might be 
"nice" but certainly not a high priority (compared to other things in the '02 
Standard - like 1-byte binary fields, bit fields, REPLACE ALSO, etc).

As far as the "debugging" facility goes (debugging lines), most (certainly not 
all) IBM mainframe people do "interactive debugging" rather than
    D DISPLAY whatever
these days.

Micro Focus first introduced THEIR conditional compilation because they used it 
internally.  The compiler itself (written in COBOL) used it so they could 
"single source" the code for various target platforms.  (Same source code did 
multiple branches of "source code streams" depending upon whether they were 
building a compiler for DOS, OS/2, HP,whomever).  Although you can do this 
checking at "run-time" - it makes (in most cases) for more efficient code if you 
only include the "relevant" source code in what gets compiled.  Using different 
COPY members is another approach used by some people, but again conditional 
compilation makes this less "error prone".

In general, it is a "nice" feature for anyone who wants to maintain a single 
source stream but compile it differently for different run-time (object code) 
variations.

P.S. One thing that really bugged me about how ANSI/ISO implemented it, was that 
they didn't include a
  >>DISPLAY
statement to let you "print" a message in the compile-time listing - so that you 
could figure out which branch of the conditional compilation you were "on".
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Conditional compliation

- **From:** "robertwessel2@yahoo.com" <robertwessel2@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2007-05-16T01:14:28-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1179303268.034652.246450@p77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<6X12i.379627$9i2.98815@fe06.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<5aqr89F2qf93pU1@mid.individual.net> <6X12i.379627$9i2.98815@fe06.news.easynews.com>`

```
On May 14, 1:32 pm, "William M. Klein" <wmkl...@nospam.netcom.com>
wrote:
> Yes, I can confirm that it is NOT in Enterprise COBOL.  I think there was
> discussion of having a SHARE requirement to add it, but that the user community
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> > definitive answer anywhere.


Many years ago we (internally) did a preprocessor (in Cobol), to
support conditional compilation.  It did the basic .IF stuff, nested
conditions, and a few other items.  Nothing too fancy, and a bit
rough, but we've been using it internally for years.  Both PC and
mainframe versions.  If people were interested, I could see if there
is interest in OSS'ing it, maybe we could do it as a SourceForge
project.

Anyway, here's some junk source code I slapped together as a sample:

       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.

           .PRINT 'DISPLAY COMMENT ON CONSOLE'

           .IF OS2
               SELECT SOURCE-R-FILE ASSIGN TO VARYING DS-SOURCE
                   ORGANIZATION IS SEQUENTIAL
                   ACCESS IS SEQUENTIAL
                   FILE STATUS IS SR-STATUS.
           .ELSE
               .SETTRUE COMPUTEDCONDITION
               SELECT SOURCE-RRR-FILE ASSIGN TO VARYING DS-SOURCE
               .IFNDEF UNIX
                   ORGANIZATION IS SEQUENTIAL
                   ACCESS IS SEQUENTIAL
                   FILE STATUS IS SRRR-STATUS.
               .ELSE
                   ORGANIZATION IS SEQUENTIAL
                   ACCESS IS SEQUENTIAL.
               .ENDIF
           .ENDIF


           05  OUTPUT-RECORD-R-2 REDEFINES OUTPUT-RECORD-TABLE.
               10  FILLER                       PIC X(06).
               10  OTR-COMMENT-1                PIC X(02).
               10  OTR-VAR-NAME                 PIC X(32).
           .IF COMPUTEDCONDITION
               10  OTR-VAR-DEF                  PIC X(12).
               10  FILLER                       PIC X(04).
               10  OTR-VAR-STATUS               PIC X(05).
               10  FILLER                       PIC X(04).
           .ENDIF
               10  OTR-USED-COUNT               PIC X(03).
               10  OTR-COMMENT-2                PIC X(02).
               10  FILLER                       PIC X(186).


That preprocesses to the following with "/ddos" specified:


       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.

      *           .PRINT 'DISPLAY COMMENT ON CONSOLE'

      * 	  .IF OS2
      *        SELECT SOURCE-R-FILE ASSIGN TO VARYING DS-SOURCE
      * 	   ORGANIZATION IS SEQUENTIAL
      * 	   ACCESS IS SEQUENTIAL
      * 	   FILE STATUS IS SR-STATUS.
      * 	  .ELSE
      * 	      .SETTRUE COMPUTEDCONDITION
	       SELECT SOURCE-RRR-FILE ASSIGN TO VARYING DS-SOURCE
      * 	      .IFNDEF UNIX
		   ORGANIZATION IS SEQUENTIAL
		   ACCESS IS SEQUENTIAL
		   FILE STATUS IS SRRR-STATUS.
      * 	      .ELSE
      * 	   ORGANIZATION IS SEQUENTIAL
      * 	   ACCESS IS SEQUENTIAL.
      * 	      .ENDIF
      * 	  .ENDIF


	   05  OUTPUT-RECORD-R-2 REDEFINES OUTPUT-RECORD-TABLE.
	       10  FILLER			PIC X(06).
	       10  OTR-COMMENT-1		PIC X(02).
	       10  OTR-VAR-NAME 		PIC X(32).
      * 	  .IF COMPUTEDCONDITION
	       10  OTR-VAR-DEF			PIC X(12).
	       10  FILLER			PIC X(04).
	       10  OTR-VAR-STATUS		PIC X(05).
	       10  FILLER			PIC X(04).
      * 	  .ENDIF
	       10  OTR-USED-COUNT		PIC X(03).
	       10  OTR-COMMENT-2		PIC X(02).
	       10  FILLER			PIC X(186).
      ****************************************************************
      **DOS				Defined 	True	   0**
      **OS2				Not defined	False	   1**
      **COMPUTEDCONDITION		Defined 	True	   1**
      **UNIX				Not defined	False	   1**
      ****************************************************************
```

## Thread: Microfocus Cobol "SLEEP" routine — 13 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Microfocus Cobol "SLEEP" routine

- **From:** "C C" <someone@atsbcglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2007-05-15T07:20:45-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yAh2i.4627$UU.2661@newssvr19.news.prodigy.net>`

```
Hello.

Is there a way in MF Cobol to pause program execution (sleep) for, let's 
say, 5 minutes???  My workaround is to call the AIX command "sleep 300".  I 
need a routine in Cobol itself so I may vary the time to pause in some 
programs.

Thanks in advance.
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus Cobol "SLEEP" routine

- **From:** "Steve Rainbird" <news.nospam@rainbird.me.nospam.uk>
- **Date:** 2007-05-15T13:52:38+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5atoomF2qeibjU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<yAh2i.4627$UU.2661@newssvr19.news.prodigy.net>`

```
"C C" <someone@atsbcglobal.net> wrote in message 
news:yAh2i.4627$UU.2661@newssvr19.news.prodigy.net...
> Hello.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Not that I know of.

I use

call "system" using ws-sleep-call


Where ws-sleep-call is defined as

      01  ws-mss-sleep-call.
           03                                 pic x(6) value "sleep ".
           03  ws-mss-sleep         pic 9(15).999.
           03                                 pic x value x"00".

Works for me.
```

Thomas

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message 
news:KVn2i.438596$c62.361592@fe07.news.easynews.com...
>I don't know which versions it is and is not in, but check your 
>documentation for the Micro Focus callable service
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Note that your application has to be threaded in order to use this API 
otherwise it returns immediately with a non-zero RETURN-CODE, as the 
CBL_THREAD_ APIs are only available in the threaded COBOL run-time.

Gael.
```

<a id="t-b05d164da8"></a>
## Thread: Acucobolo beginner ... — 1 this month / 3 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

_Thread started [2007-03](2007-03.md#t-b05d164da8)._

#### ↳ Re: Acucobolo beginner ...

- **From:** john.paura@gmail.com
- **Date:** 2007-05-16T07:34:54-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1179326094.832299.88090@k79g2000hse.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<45fda33f$0$4794$4fafbaef@reader4.news.tin.it>`
- **References:** `<45fda33f$0$4794$4fafbaef@reader4.news.tin.it>`

```
On 18 Mrz., 16:38, "Daniele" <xxx....@zzz.it> wrote:
> Hi to evrybody,
>      j'm moving my very very first step in cobol .... using acucobol.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Than's for all, Daniele

Hi Danielle,
One very important item to keep in mind about AcuCobol is that there
are two ways to accomplish nearly every program - you can code
everything longhand in traditional COBOL methods or you can use the
code generator which is core to AcuCobol's existence.  I use both way
and am partial to the traditional method.  For what you want to do,
the easiest method is to use the code generator.  There are still a
few ways to do this but what you could do is define an event to the
button you are coding for - in the Structural View choose the screen
you are coding for then click lightly on the button you've defined, go
to the event tab of the properties window and select the type of
event, set up a paragraph name for the event and the event section
will open with the cursor underneath the paragraph title you've
defined.  Here you can put traditional code in to accomplish the
calling of your program.  Good luck.  (Sometimes the automatic code
generation can be frustrating to work with!)
```

---

<a id="t-181f5ff215"></a>
## Thread: Recent book published covering COBOL — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Recent book published covering COBOL

- **From:** yyyc186@hughes.net
- **Date:** 2007-05-16T16:38:25-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1179358705.831530.17060@u30g2000hsc.googlegroups.com>`

```
Logikal Solutions has released "The Minimum You Need to Know to Be an
OpenVMS Application Developer"  ISBN 0-9770866-0-7.

This book covers developing the same application in BASIC, FORTRAN,
COBOL and C/C++.  The use of RMS indexed files and RDB Database.
MySQL is also covered, but only from a C perspective.  The FMS screen
management library, MMS/CMS, DCL and a host of other concepts.  It is
designed so a person familiar with one of the covered languages can
both quickly come up to speed on OpenVMS, and learn another of the
covered languages by comparing the programs.  You can learn more about
this and other books from Logikal Solutions by visiting
http://www.theminimumyouneedtoknow.com.

Thank you for your time
```

<a id="t-1513616f89"></a>
## Thread: COBOL Standards — 16 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Re: COBOL Standards

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2007-05-17T19:20:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KV13i.191791$aG1.172361@pd7urf3no>`

```
Further to my previous message about COBOL Standards, I'm highlighting 
the following WITHOUT comment :-

J4/07-0071 - Draft Minutes of Meeting 261 for Apr 16 - 20, 2007.
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

13.2     Planning for WG4

13.2.1    07-0064 - Resources, responsibilities and priorities (Klein)

The committee directed Ms. Bennett to prepare a document addressing 
status, resources, and options for COBOL standardization efforts.

  Mr. Schricker will prepare a liaison report from J4 to WG4.

J4 recommends that the following features in the 2002 standard be made 
optional:

ï¿½         report writer

ï¿½         validate

ï¿½         standard arithmetic (as in the 2002 standard)

ï¿½         screen handling facility

  J4 recommends that the following new features of the current draft be 
optional

ï¿½         collection classes

ï¿½         finalizer

ï¿½         dynamic-capacity tables

  J4 recommends that the following new features of the current draft be 
kept as core features:

ï¿½         XML

ï¿½         any-length elementary items


With respect to method overloading, J4 recommends it be retained in the 
draft.

 
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Standards

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-05-18T10:26:06+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5b433uF2qk6lqU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<KV13i.191791$aG1.172361@pd7urf3no>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
news:KV13i.191791$aG1.172361@pd7urf3no...
> Further to my previous message about COBOL Standards, I'm highlighting the 
> following WITHOUT comment :-
…[45 more quoted lines elided]…
> --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

While I admire your restraint, Jimmy, I'm certainly going to comment... :-)

It is becoming perfectly obvious that features proposed by the  J4 Chair 
will be "recommended" for incorporation. Everything else is not important. 
This same Chair accepted an award for delivering the 2002 COBOL standard 
only 17 years late, when an honest person would have declined and apologised 
to the COBOL community.

Perhaps I would be less cynical about this if there had been some 
explanation as to why the above choices were made. Of course, if you don't 
have to answer to anyone, then there's absolutely no need to explain 
yourself.

Bit like Mary Poppins, really... ("I never explain anything").   Fine, if 
you have mastered sliding up the banisters or floating away on an umbrella 
when the wind changes; not fine if you haven't.

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2007-05-17T22:31:40-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<134q48p9vt2a647@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<KV13i.191791$aG1.172361@pd7urf3no> <5b433uF2qk6lqU1@mid.individual.net>`

```

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:5b433uF2qk6lqU1@mid.individual.net...
[snip]
> This same Chair accepted an award for delivering the 2002 COBOL standard
> only 17 years late, when an honest person would have declined and
apologised

Mr Dashwood, you seem to be using an idiosyncratic
definition for "late", or you misspoke.

Work for the 2002 standard appears to have begun
around 1991-1992 and was initially scheduled for
release in 1997. (That is only five years late by my
reckoning.)

However, as I understand it, the object-oriented
features were initially based on Smalltalk, which was
not approved as a standard until 1998; but some,
I believe, wanted more compatibility with C++, which,
also, was not approved as a standard until 1998.
(That suggests no more that four years late, if one can
be satisfied only with instant gratification.)

I believe that, as a practical matter and considering
all that was added, the 2002 standard was not late at all.
And, considering the problems I have seen with that
standard (remember that I have been diagnosed with
OCD), I feel it may have been released a bit early. <g>
[There are more than 50 defect reports and a number
of editorial corrections for the 2002 standard.]
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<134s1h3jfp1fn55@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<KV13i.191791$aG1.172361@pd7urf3no> <5b433uF2qk6lqU1@mid.individual.net> <134q48p9vt2a647@corp.supernews.com> <aBk3i.194158$6m4.110953@pd7urf1no>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:aBk3i.194158$6m4.110953@pd7urf1no...
[snip]
> I checked on OCD Rick. My sympathies. You're a brave man and still have
> your sense of humour.

I am not certain how bavery is involved; but I think
having a sense of humor has helped me deal with
some sources of depression. It is difficult to be
depressed when laughing. <g>

Here is one that bothers me in the current COBOL
standard, draft, and DF-52. There are several
references to "level 1 data description entry"; however
data description entries do not have levels, they have
level numbers. According to the COBOL standard
style guide, each of these should read "level-number 1
data description entry". [Record description entries
have levels that are determined by the level-numbers
of the data description entries comprising the record
description entry.]

> But I think you've got it wrong about Smalltalk. I googled on
> 'smalltalk-history'. Strictly speaking,(if you read some of the
> articles), Smalltalk was up and running as of Smalltalk 76. It seems
> Adele Goldberg was one of the main instigators for the Smalltalk-80
> standard, (introduced 80-83).

I used Google with 'smalltalk standard' and found
< http://www.smalltalk.org/versions >, which says,
in part,
-----
ANSI Smalltalk Standard
"The growth, spread and potential of Smalltalk led to
a need for a standard that will protect the users' interest
in compatibility and portability. The J20 technical
committee was formed in the summer of 1993 to
develop the ANSI Smalltalk standard."

"The ANSI Smalltalk standard was approved on
May 19, 1998."
-----

Smalltalk-80 is also mentioned on this web page.

The intended comparison was with the output of
standards committees, e.g., J4 vs. J20, for production
of a standards including object-orientation. Everything
prior to the ANSI version were de facto standards.
With ANSI standardization, Smalltalk 98 became a
de jure standard, in the US. (I did not check to see
whether it is also an ISO standard.)

< http://www.itl.nist.gov/fipspubs/withdraw.htm >
-----
It is no longer necessary for the government to mandate
standards that duplicate industry standards. Federal
government departments and agencies are directed by
the National Technology Transfer and Advancement
Act of 1995 (P.L. 104-113), to use technical industry
standards that are developed in voluntary consensus
standards bodies.
-----
[This page also mention the withdrawal of FIPS
for COBOL 85 effective February 25, 2000.]
```
